This is Lesson 19's "events are the ledger, never rewritten" dividend cashed at the context layer: making environment changes in-place events equips the session's "environment dimension" too with a trustworthy, replayable history, rather than only a "what's current" instantaneous snapshot that lost its past. Being able to faithfully replay the environment of any past moment is the least noticeable yet most precious ability bought by "weaving context into the event stream."

❌ pinned to the prompt's title page

Stitch "current environment" to the front each round. The model knows only "what's now," not "when it changed" — the timeline flattened, earlier messages' context lost.

✅ in-place System message

The change is inserted by seq at the point it happened. Reading history, the model sees the timing of environment changes clearly, every message's context lining up.

🗺️ The big picture

This lesson catches Lesson 24's ContextUpdated event and explains how it reaches the model:

  • System message = a context update's avatar: its text type directly reuses ContextUpdated.text — the event is a ledger entry, the System message its look in projected history.
  • The same projection pipeline: ContextUpdated is just an ordinary member alongside Text/Tool/Reasoning in the projector, no special channel — "make the special unspecial," automatically inheriting durable/ordered/replayable/in-place.
  • Position is meaning: the System message inserted in place by seq preserves the timing of environment changes; the model can thus correctly understand earlier messages' context (vs "pinned to the title page," which flattens the timeline).

By here, the context epoch's "normal operation + mid-conversation update" is covered. Part 5 has only two puzzle pieces left: Lesson 26 looks at what the concrete built-in sources (environment, date, instructions) actually look like and how each is implemented; Lesson 27 cracks the one hard bone left unopened — the AgentReplacementBlocked on agent switch, that "changing of the guard" puzzle Lesson 24 kept naming but never unpacked.

🔬 Source detail

The System message's text reusing ContextUpdated's text, and its line in the projector alongside many events:

// session/message.ts — text type borrowed directly from the event
text: SessionEvent.ContextUpdated.data.fields.text

// session/projector.ts — ContextUpdated registered alongside Text/Tool/…
yield* events.project(SessionEvent.ContextUpdated, (event) => run(db, event))
yield* events.project(SessionEvent.Synthetic, ...)
yield* events.project(SessionEvent.Text.Started, ...)
yield* events.project(SessionEvent.Tool.Called, ...)   // ← all the same project treatment

The first line uses SessionEvent.ContextUpdated.data.fields.text to borrow the event's field type for the System message's text — a very opencode move: rather than redeclaring "text is a string," point directly at that field in the event, letting the type system guarantee "the message's text is forever the same shape as the event's text." The string of project registrations below is plain to see: ContextUpdated sits side by side with Text, Tool — projecting into a message goes through the same run(db, event) as they do. (Strictly, ContextUpdated's handler has one extra replay branch beyond plain run: on replay it also calls requestReplacement; but the main road, "projecting into a System message," does share the same mechanism as Text/Tool.) The code's "unremarkableness" is precisely the evidence of the design's "wholeness."

🎯 Key points
  • Lesson 24's ContextUpdated event is projected into a System message (a member of Lesson 14's Message union), inserted in place at its seq position.
  • System.text reuses ContextUpdated.text: a System message is a context update's "avatar" in projected history (a concrete instance of Lesson 19's event→projection).
  • The same projection pipeline: ContextUpdated sits alongside Text/Tool/Reasoning in the projector, no special channel — "make the special unspecial," automatically inheriting durable/ordered/replayable/in-place.
  • Position is meaning: in-place insertion preserves the timing of environment changes, so the model correctly understands earlier messages' context — vs "pinned to the title page," which flattens the timeline and loses causality.
  • Design philosophy: everything belongs to one event timeline — context updates aren't a side special case but a first-class citizen of the conversation stream.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In what form does Lesson 24's ContextUpdated event ultimately reach the model?
  1. Stitched to the front of the system prompt
  2. Through a side injector
  3. Projected into a System message, inserted in place by seq at the moment it happened
  4. Printed to the server log
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. A System message (one of Lesson 14's Message union) reuses ContextUpdated.text as its text type. reconcile's diff → ContextUpdated event → projected into a System message → lands in place by seq. A System message is a context update's avatar in projected history.
2. When exactly is a context change injected into history?
  1. Pushed into history asynchronously the instant a source changes
  2. Sampled lazily, admitted only at a "safe provider-turn boundary" (before a provider call, after input promotion + tool settlement), never pushed asynchronously
  3. The model decides when to read it
  4. Filled in all at once when the session ends
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Async pushing breeds chaos (the model may be mid-stream). opencode defers sampling to the clean gap "just before the next provider call": settle just-promoted user input/settled tool results first, then sample whether sources changed → if so admit one; multiple changes at one boundary combine into one. Exactly Lesson 15's steer "takes effect at a safe boundary" discipline.
3. Why is the System message being "inserted in place" (vs pinned to the prompt's title page) so important?
  1. In-place insertion saves tokens
  2. To make history shorter
  3. Position carries timing, timing carries meaning: in-place preserves when the change happened, so the model understands earlier messages' context
  4. Purely for nice formatting
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. At msg6 "run tests" in /proj, msg7 cd to /src, msg8 "again." The two may differ. In-place System messages show the model the switch timing; pinning only "current /src" at the front, the model doesn't know it changed at msg7 and misreads msg6's "tests." Position=timing=meaning. It also makes the environment's evolution precisely replayable.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson clarifies: the System message here isn't the opening "system prompt" defining the AI's persona, but "stage directions" inserted mid-conversation as the environment changes (the opening setup actually lands in the baseline). With the "title-page note vs mid-show stage direction" metaphor, explain why the former doesn't care about position while the latter is all about timing.
  • The lesson says "as long as you can express something as an event with a seq, it can automatically, in-place, replayably appear in the history the model reads." Based on this, imagine a new kind of "mid-conversation info" opencode doesn't yet have but could plug in the same way, and describe how it would travel this pipeline.

Built-in sources

Lessons 21 to 25 built a rather precise machine: the source algebra, codec-derived equality, the registry, the context epoch, in-place System messages… all about "mechanism." This lesson, at last, is time to lift the lid and see what parts are actually inside — what opencode's truly built-in context sources actually look like. And the most knowing-smile discovery is: they're unbelievably simple. A source's complete definition is often a dozen lines. The seemingly-complex machinery of the past five lessons, at the concrete source, dissolves into a few crisp declarations. What this lesson wants you to feel is the sweetest reward of "good abstraction": absorb the complexity into the framework, leave the use site simple to near-transparency.

Why call out "sources are this simple"? Because it's the touchstone of whether an abstraction succeeds. A bad abstraction makes "add a new thing" tedious — you must know a heap of internal detail, carefully wire up various connections. A good abstraction makes "add a new thing" nearly fill-in-the-blank: you only declare the bit of info unique to this thing, and the rest — serialization, equality, registration, refresh, projection — the framework wraps up for you. Through two real built-in sources, core/environment and core/date, this lesson lets you see firsthand: to what degree the past five lessons' machinery simplifies "writing a context source."

🧩 Analogy
Imagine you spent great effort building a "smart form system": it auto-validates, auto-stores version history, auto-highlights "what changed since last time." The system itself is complex — but precisely because it's complex, actually filling in a form is dead simple. To fill the "current location form," you just write one line "Location: Beijing"; the "date form," just "Today: Monday." Validation, archiving, comparison, change-flagging — these the form system silently does behind the scenes, and the form-filler needn't worry about any of it. opencode's built-in sources are just such "filled-in forms": core/environment fills "directory, project root, is-git-repo," core/date fills "what's today." The forms are pitifully short, because the past five lessons' "form system" (the source framework) long since carried off all the heavy lifting. The simpler the form, the more it proves how powerful the system is.

core/environment: the environment you're in

The first built-in source is core/environment — it tells the model "what runtime environment you're in right now." The value it observes is an environment description wrapped in <env> tags:

// simplified from system-context/builtins.ts
const environment = [
  "<env>",
  `  Working directory: ${'$'}{location.directory}`,        // working directory
  `  Workspace root folder: ${'$'}{location.project.directory}`, // project root
  `  Is directory a git repo: ${'$'}{...}`,                  // is it a git repo
  `  Platform: ${'$'}{process.platform}`,                    // OS platform
  "</env>",
].join("\n")

Note the <env>...</env> tags — a common trick for feeding structured info to an LLM: XML-like tags draw clear boundaries around a block of info, so the model recognizes "this is the environment block" at a glance, not confusing it with the body. The four items inside are also chosen practically: working directory (where I work), project root (where this project's boundary is), is-git-repo (whether git commands are usable), platform (Linux or Mac, affecting command syntax). These four are exactly the bare minimum an agent meant to write code and run commands for you must know about "where am I."

The selection of these four itself shows the designer's grasp of "what an agent truly needs." It does not dump the whole system's environment variables, disk capacity, CPU model at the model — mostly noise that only crowds the precious context and distracts the model. What it handpicks is the few that genuinely affect the agent's decisions: knowing the working directory, relative paths compute right; knowing the project root, it won't edit files outside the project; knowing is-git-repo, it decides whether to use git for diffs; knowing the platform, it knows to write ls or dir. Context given to the model is about quality, not quantity — each item should be one where "not giving it would cause mistakes," not "no harm giving it." This restraint is of one piece with Lesson 18's tool output to disk, and the whole system's reverence for "tokens are precious."

Working directory
current dir — where commands run, where relative paths compute from
Workspace root
project root directory — the project's boundary
Is git repo
whether git is usable (affects the agent's choices)
Platform
linux/darwin/win32 — command syntax varies by platform

core/date: even "the date" is a source

The second built-in source core/date is simple to a single load line: get today's date. But its most intriguing point is exactly a question: why make "the date," of all things, a source with baseline/update, rather than just hard-code "today is X" at the start and be done?

// simplified from system-context/builtins.ts — a source, under ten lines
SystemContext.make({
  key: SystemContext.Key.make("core/date"),
  codec: Schema.toCodecJson(Schema.String),
  load: DateTime.nowAsDate.pipe(Effect.map((d) => d.toDateString())),
  baseline: (date) => `Today's date: ${'$'}{date}`,            // first time
  update: (_prev, date) => `Today's date is now: ${'$'}{date}`,  // after crossing a day
})

The answer: because the date changes. A session may chat from evening into the next morning — crossing midnight. If the date is hard-coded at the start, then past midnight the model's "today" is wrong, possibly mistaking yesterday for today, miscounting a deadline by a day. Make the date a source, and the next round's prepare reconcile finds "the date changed," auto-publishing an update — so the model reads "Today's date is now: ...," the clock seamlessly corrected. Even "the date," seemingly unchanging, is "alive" in a possibly-long session — exactly the meaning of the past five lessons' incremental mechanism: it doesn't care about size, any "possibly-changing" environment info can be kept fresh elegantly by the same source framework.

Mon 21:00session starts → baseline: "Today's date: Mon Jun 23"
Mon 23:30still chatting → reconcile: date unchanged → Unchanged, silent
★ midnight 00:00the date changed!
Tue 00:15next round → reconcile: changed → update: "Today's date is now: Tue Jun 24"

baseline: "Today's date: X"

First notification. The wording reads like stating a fact.

update: "Today's date is now: X"

After crossing a day. That now signals to the model "this is a change."

Savor the wording difference between baseline and update: first is "Today's date: X" (flatly stated), after change is "Today's date is now: X" — the extra now gently reminds the model "note, this is newly changed." Same on the environment source: baseline says "Here is some useful information…," update says "The environment …is now:". This little care of using the wording itself to distinguish "initial" from "change" seems trivial but makes the model's reading more natural — it senses from between the lines "oh, this changed later," rather than yet another repetition. Good context isn't only correct in content but correct in the tone of "is this new news."

This detail is so small almost no one would notice, yet it's the purest expression of "considering the reader" — only here the "reader" is an LLM. A careless implementation could use identical wording for baseline and update; the info gets through either way. But opencode spent this extra care: making the "first statement" read like a statement, the "subsequent change" read like a change. To the model, "now" is a faint but clear signal helping it separate "a restatement of the current state" from "a change just happened." Keeping the user's (even an AI's) reading experience in mind, refusing to be casual about even a single word's choice — this near-obsessive thoughtfulness is often the subtle, decisive watershed between "works" and "works well."

And instructions: InstructionContext

Beyond environment and date, there's a "heavier" built-in source — InstructionContext (instruction-context.ts). It handles the project's standing instructions: files like AGENTS.md, stating "what conventions this project uses, what's taboo, how it wants the agent to work." These instructions are also wrapped into a source, registered into the same registry — so it participates in baseline, increment, projection on equal footing with environment and date. Change the project conventions (you edit AGENTS.md), and it too can be incrementally told to the model, just like a directory change. (One implementation detail: core/environment and core/date are first combined into one and registered under a single key core/builtins, while InstructionContext is a separate key — so the registry actually holds two entries. But because combine preserves each source's own snapshot, environment and date are still observed and incremented independently, logically still three sources.)

Put these three sources together and opencode's "world fed to the model" has flesh and blood: environment tells it "where I am, what platform," date tells it "when it is now," instructions tell it "this project's rules." Each is an independent source, each observing, each incrementing, each updating in place, finally gathered by the registry into one complete, ordered, incrementable system context. This is the full look of the past five lessons' abstraction landed in practice.

One more thing to mull: these three sources are opencode's built-ins, but they stand on completely equal footing with "a source some plugin contributes in future." core/environment enjoys no privilege for being surnamed "core" — it too dutifully makes a Source and registers into the same registry. This means: what opencode uses itself and what it opens to third-party extension are the same mechanism. This "insiders and outsiders eat from the same pot" design (the industry calls it dogfooding — eating your own dog food) is the best proof of a healthy extension mechanism: because the framework's authors live on it themselves, it can't be a perfunctory, second-class extension point. Want to add a "current branch name" or "last build status" context source to opencode in future? You'd write the exact same five lines as core/date — that's the most generous promise of a good abstraction.

core/environment
"where I am": directory, project root, git, platform
core/date
"when it is now": today's date (updates across days)
InstructionContext
"the rules here": standing instructions like AGENTS.md
🗺️ The big picture

This lesson cashes the first five lessons' abstraction into a few real, minimal sources:

  • Source definitions are astonishingly short: a dozen lines declaring key/codec/load/baseline/update, the heavy lifting all absorbed by the framework (codec-derived equality, registry, epoch, projection) — the touchstone of "good abstraction."
  • core/environment: <env> tags wrapping directory/project-root/git/platform — the bare minimum an agent must know about "where am I."
  • core/date: even "the date" is a source, because a session may cross midnight — any "possibly-changing" info deserves keeping fresh by the source framework.
  • Wording distinction: baseline flat, update uses "now" to lightly flag "this is a change" — even the tone considers the model.
  • InstructionContext: project instructions like AGENTS.md are also an equal-footing source.

By here, system context's "normal state" is fully covered: abstraction (L21-23), landing in the session (L24), mid-conversation update (L25), concrete built-ins (this lesson). Part 5 has only the last and hardest puzzle piece left — the AgentReplacementBlocked Lesson 24 kept naming but never unpacked: when you switch an agent mid-way, what becomes of the context epoch belonging to the old agent? Why is "replacement" sometimes "blocked"? That's the next lesson, Part 5's finale — Agent switch & epoch replacement (Lesson 27).

🔬 Source detail

How simple is a complete source definition? See core/environment in full — five fields, not a wasted word:

// simplified from system-context/builtins.ts
SystemContext.make({
  key: SystemContext.Key.make("core/environment"),     // unique identity
  codec: Schema.toCodecJson(Schema.String),               // value is a string
  load: Effect.succeed(environment),                      // how to observe
  baseline: (env) => `Here is ...:\n${'$'}{env}`,            // first-time full text
  update: (_prev, env) => `The environment ...is now:\n${'$'}{env}`, // change text
})

Just these five lines. Not a line writing "how to serialize" (one point to a codec, and Lesson 22's encode/decode/equivalent fall into place automatically); not a line writing "how to register, sort, parallelize" (handed to Lesson 23's registry); not a line writing "how to persist, pin a sequence, project into a System message" (handed to Lessons 24, 25). A source author need only answer five most-essential questions: what it's called, what the value looks like, how to observe, how to say it first time, how to say it on change. Everything else, the past five lessons' machinery answers for them. The crispness of these five lines is bought by all the prior complexity — complexity absorbed into the framework, simplicity left for the user.

what the framework carries (unseen)

encode/decode/equivalent equality, scoped registration, sort by key, concurrent observe, epoch persistence, pin baselineSeq, project into a System message, in-place positioning… a whole heap.

what the source author writes (just five lines)

key, codec, load, baseline, update. Answer "name/value shape/how to observe/first-time/on-change," done.

key
"core/date" — unique identity
codec
Schema.String — value is a string
load
get today's date — how to observe
baseline / update
"Today's date: X" / "…is now: X" — first-time / on-change
🎯 Key points
  • The first five lessons' abstraction cashes into real sources that are astonishingly simple: a dozen lines declaring key/codec/load/baseline/update, the heavy lifting all absorbed by the framework — the touchstone of "good abstraction."
  • core/environment: <env> tags wrapping directory, project root, is-git, platform — the bare minimum an agent must know about "where am I."
  • core/date: even "the date" is a source, because a session may cross midnight — any "possibly-changing" info deserves keeping fresh by the source framework.
  • Wording distinguishes initial from change: baseline "Today's date: X" flat, update "Today's date is now: X" with "now" lightly flagging the change — even the tone considers the model.
  • InstructionContext (project instructions like AGENTS.md) is also an equal-footing source: environment "where I am," date "when," instructions "the rules" — together weaving the world the model is in.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why is even "the date" made a source with baseline/update?
  1. Purely to demo the framework
  2. Because the date changes — a session may cross midnight; after crossing, reconcile auto-sends an update, correcting the model's "today"
  3. To fill up the context window
  4. Because the date is important
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. A session may chat from evening into early morning, crossing midnight. Hard-coded at start, past midnight the model's "today" is wrong (yesterday as today, miscounted deadline). As a source, next round's reconcile finds the change and publishes "Today's date is now:…". Any possibly-changing info deserves staying fresh via the source framework.
2. Built-in source definitions are often just a dozen lines — what does this show?
  1. It shows date and environment don't matter
  2. The touchstone of good abstraction: complexity absorbed into the framework, the source author declares only five essentials — key/codec/load/baseline/update
  3. It shows the code is unfinished
  4. It shows these sources are weak
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. No line writes "how to serialize/register/sort/parallelize/persist/pin-seq/project into a System message" — all absorbed by the past five lessons' framework. The author answers five essentials: name/value shape/how to observe/first-time/on-change. Adding a thing being near fill-in-the-blank = good abstraction; tedious = bad.
3. Why does core/environment pick only directory/root/git/platform, not dump all environment variables at the model?
  1. Because other env vars can't be read
  2. Context for the model is about quality not quantity — give only what "would cause mistakes if omitted"; extra info is noise, crowds tokens, distracts
  3. Picked at random, no reason
  4. To make the code shorter
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Each of the four affects agent decisions: working dir→relative paths, project root→don't edit outside, is-git→whether to use git, platform→ls or dir. Other env vars are mostly noise crowding precious context and distracting. Quality not quantity, of one piece with Lesson 18's reverence for precious tokens.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says core built-in sources stand on equal footing with "sources future plugins contribute" (dogfooding) — the framework authors live on it themselves, so the extension point can't be second-class. Why is "insiders and outsiders using the same mechanism" the best proof of a healthy extension mechanism?
  • The lesson praises baseline/update using the word "now" to hint "this is a change" — a detail almost no one notices. From tools you've used or prompts you've read, give an example where "a bit more care in wording for the reader (even an AI)" noticeably improved the experience.

Agent switch & epoch

All through Part 5, one name was repeatedly invoked yet never unpacked — AgentReplacementBlocked. Lesson 24 on epochs said "switching agent takes replace, may be blocked"; Lessons 25 and 26 twice teased "this is the finale's hard bone." Now, time to crack it. This lesson, also Part 5's finale, answers a question that sounds simple but is subtle to do: when you switch an agent mid-session, what becomes of the context epoch built for the old agent? Why is "replacement" sometimes "blocked"? The answer illuminates, at once, Lesson 21's seemingly-unremarkable unavailable ≠ removed principle and the whole system's character of "rather block than be incomplete." Reading it, you don't just understand an edge mechanism but touch opencode's near-stubborn prudence toward "foundational changes."

Why is "switching agent" a hard bone needing careful handling? Because it demands rebuilding a complete baseline from scratch. Lesson 24 covered: a same agent's environment evolution takes reconcile (increment); but switch the agent and the old baseline's "persona" is wholly void, requiring replace — re-observe all sources, stitch a new baseline from scratch. And "stitch a complete baseline from scratch" has a strict precondition: all sources must be observable right now. What if a source happens to be temporarily down (Lesson 21's unavailable)? This lesson's whole tension lives in that "what if."

🏔️ Analogy
Continuing Lesson 24's mountaineering metaphor: switching agents is switching to a wholly new climbing team, and the new team needs its own base camp (new epoch). But to pitch a new base camp, you must first do a complete survey of the surroundings — altitude, coordinates, weather, supply points, not one missing (re-observe all sources). Now imagine: just as you're about to pitch the new camp, the GPS temporarily loses signal (a source unavailable). What do you do? A responsible leader would never pitch camp on half a coordinate — that's housing the new team at a site with no standard location, trouble sooner or later. They'd pause pitching, wait for GPS to recover; meanwhile, the old team's base camp stays valid, still on standby. That's the whole spirit of AgentReplacementBlocked: rather than build a new baseline missing a piece, block, wait, keep the old. A delayed handover beats a new camp built on incomplete data.

reconcile or replace: two magnitudes of change

First nail down Lesson 24's fork again, as it's this lesson's starting point. Each prepare reconcile, stored.agent (the archive's agent) compared with the current agent decides which road:

same agent → reconcile

The "persona" unchanged, just the environment drifted a bit. Compute the increment on the old baseline, append an update. Normal operation.

switch agent → replace

The "persona" wholly changed, the old baseline void. Must re-observe all sources, stitch a new baseline from scratch. Changing of the guard.

The key difference is the two roads' tolerance for "a source unobservable" is utterly different. reconcile is forgiving: a source unavailable right now? Fine, it keeps the last snapshot — the baseline still stands, missing this round's observation does no harm (exactly Lesson 21's unavailable ≠ removed in normal operation). But replace has no such ease: it stitches a brand-new baseline, with no "last snapshot" to fall back on — every source must be observed now, on the spot, freshly to stitch a complete, self-consistent new baseline. One can tolerate "temporarily unseen," one can't — the difference explodes right here.

Pause on this contrast and you'll better grasp the weight of "whether context has continuity." reconcile can tolerate one absence because it stands on the shoulders of an existing baseline — the old snapshot is its safety net, miss this round and catch up next, the baseline always complete. But replace starts a new fire, with no old baseline underfoot, a true "from scratch." "From scratch" most fears "missing material at the open": an increment missing one entry just catches up a round later; but laying a foundation missing a piece welds the gap into the bedrock, carrying that hole every round after. So the same "a source unread" is trivial in the increment scenario, grave in the foundation scenario — opencode treating the two differently is exactly seeing through that "laying a foundation" and "adding bricks" are fundamentally not the same thing.

Why block: refusing an incomplete new baseline

So SystemContext.replace's logic has that crucial gate. It re-observes all sources, then makes a judgment:

// simplified from replaceObservation in system-context/index.ts
if (entries.some((e) =>
    e._tag === "Unavailable" && getSnapshot(previous, e.key) !== undefined))
  return { _tag: "ReplacementBlocked" }   // ← once present, now unseen → block
return { _tag: "ReplacementReady", generation: ... }  // all observed → proceed

Reading this judgment, the cleverness is all in the condition: a source is now Unavailable, AND it was present in the old snapshot. Both conditions are indispensable. If a source never existed (not in the old snapshot either), its absence now is fine; but if a source should be there yet is temporarily unobservable, that's dangerous — forcing a new baseline would silently erase this source from the new agent's world. The new agent would think "this isn't a git repo," "there are no project instructions," when really that one fetch just failed. opencode's choice is resolute: in this case, rather ReplacementBlocked, blocking this switch, than deliver a new baseline missing a source.

❌ make do: stitch anyway

Source unread? Treat it as absent, stitch the new baseline anyway. The new agent is silently fed an incomplete world — thinks "not a git repo," makes wrong decisions. The error is hidden, hard to trace.

✅ block: build only when complete

A source missing → block, keep the old epoch, retry when it recovers. The cost is a slightly delayed switch; the gain is a new baseline always complete and trustworthy. Slow over wrong.

switch agentpersona void
replace →
re-observe all sourcesstitch from scratch
a source missing?
Blocked / Readyblock+retry / proceed to new epoch
1switch agent → replace: re-observe all sources
2check: any source "present in old snapshot but now Unavailable"?
3ayes → ReplacementBlocked → throw AgentReplacementBlocked, old epoch kept, await retry
3bno (all observed) → ReplacementReady → stitch new baseline, pin new baselineSeq, revision+1

This gate is the ultimate cash-out of Lesson 21's unavailable ≠ removed principle. We said then "temporarily missing isn't deleted," sounding like an inconsequential design fastidiousness; now you see its real teeth: precisely because the system strictly distinguishes "temporarily unseen" from "really absent" from the start, it can make the right call of "wait a moment" rather than "make do" at the most critical juncture of switching agents. A principle planted early bears its most important fruit, six lessons later, in a most subtle scenario. This is the compound interest of good design: that early bit of restraint becomes, now, confidence you needn't think about.

fence: making the switch atomic, consistent

Whichever outcome the replacement heads toward, there's one last discipline to keep: this switch must be atomic and consistent — never half-switched then disrupted by another concurrent operation. Keeping it is the revision version number running throughout, plus a checking move called fence.

fence, before writing, double-checks: the agent in the current archive, is it still the one I thought? The revision version, still the one I read? If even one mismatches, it means while I was planning this switch, someone else already acted — so it dies with a RevisionMismatch/AgentMismatch, caught by the outer retryRevisionMismatch, which backs off and retries. This optimistic concurrency of "compare versions, redo on mismatch" ensures that even if two places try to switch agents almost simultaneously, only one cleanly settles in the end, never leaving a half-switched, garbled epoch. (By the way: fence actually guards the no-write branches like "blocked / unchanged"; the path that really writes the new baseline is guarded by replace's own transactional WHERE revision = expected check — different routes, same idea: revision as the token.)

t0you switch agent from A to B → prepare takes replace
t1a source happens to be down → ReplacementBlocked → throw AgentReplacementBlocked
t1~t2the old epoch (A's) stays valid, still on standby — the session never enters an incomplete state
t2source recovers → next prepare retries replace → ReplacementReady → B's new epoch settles cleanly
ReplacementReady
all sources observed → stitch new baseline, replace, revision+1
ReplacementBlocked
a source missing → AgentReplacementBlocked, keep old epoch, await retry
fence + revision
double-check agent/version before write, mismatch → back off and retry — atomic, consistent switch

Put block and fence together and you find opencode keeps two defenses for "switching agents": one against incompleteness (block: don't build a new baseline if sources aren't all there), one against garbling (fence: someone interfered, back off and redo). Both defenses point to one belief — an epoch, this "foundational state of the session," is either established completely and consistently, or not touched at all; never is a half-baked, self-contradictory middle state permitted. This is exactly that main line running through all of Parts 4 and 5 (persist first, atomic commit, serial drain, version guarding…) echoing one last time in the finale scenario of "switching agents."

Stepping way back, what this lesson truly teaches you may not be the concrete mechanism AgentReplacementBlocked but the zero tolerance for "middle states" behind it. Across Parts 4 and 5, you'll find opencode repeatedly doing the same thing: wherever a "foundational state change" is involved — admitting a prompt, advancing an epoch, switching an agent — it insists "either complete success, or clean no-change," never leaving an awkward "half-succeeded" wreck. This stubbornness is exactly the deepest divide between a "reliable system" and a "script that runs": a script cares only "can it run in the normal case," while a reliable system spends great effort on "in the abnormal case, can it gracefully break nothing." AgentReplacementBlocked is but this stubbornness's last footnote, left as Part 5 takes its bow.

🗺️ The big picture · Part 5 finale

This lesson cracks AgentReplacementBlocked and makes all of Part 5 (the Context Epoch system) close up. Look back along the main line:

  • Lessons 21-23: system context's "production side" — the source algebra, a single source's observe/compare/render, the registry.
  • Lesson 24: the context epoch — nailing the baseline into a baselineSeq-numbered anchor in the session (closing Lesson 19).
  • Lesson 25: mid-conversation updates as in-place System messages, seamlessly woven into the conversation timeline.
  • Lesson 26: the minimal built-in sources (environment/date/instructions) — the touchstone of good abstraction.
  • Lesson 27 · this one: switching agent takes replace; sources incomplete → block (the ultimate cash-out of unavailable≠removed), fence+revision keep the switch atomic and consistent.

One line for Part 5: opencode makes "what world the model should see each round" into a composable, incrementable, persistable, evolvable "context epoch" system — sharing one event timeline with the session engine, rather blocking than incomplete, rather retrying than garbled. The engine (Part 4) is already running, the brain deciding "what world the engine should read" (Part 5) fully covered. Next, Part 6, we dive deeper — the LLM protocol layer: the precise adapter system translating the model's requests and responses into each provider's (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini…) own dialect.

🔬 Source detail

prepareOnce's "switch agent blocked" branch nails this lesson's logic plainly:

// simplified from prepareOnce in session/context-epoch.ts
const result = stored.replacement_seq === null && !replacingAgent
  ? yield* SystemContext.reconcile(value, snapshot)   // same agent: increment
  : yield* SystemContext.replace(value, snapshot)     // switch agent: rebuild
if (result._tag === "ReplacementBlocked" && replacingAgent) {
  yield* fence(db, sessionID, agent, stored.revision)        // guard consistency
  return yield* new AgentReplacementBlocked({          // throw typed error
    sessionID, previous: stored.agent, current: agent })
}

Note the fields the AgentReplacementBlocked error carries: previous (the original agent) and current (the agent wanted). It's not a vague "switch failed" but states plainly "who wanted to switch to whom, but didn't" — again Lessons 5, 8, 20's worldview of "errors are values, fully informed." The upper layer, receiving this error, knows clearly "the switch from A to B was blocked," and can decide to retry later or prompt the user. Even a corner case like "being blocked" is folded into the type, fully informed — this is the whole system's consistent insistence that "failure too must be stated clearly."

🎯 Key points
  • Switching agent takes replace (not reconcile): the old baseline's "persona" is void, requiring re-observing all sources and stitching a new baseline from scratch (Lesson 24).
  • The key difference: reconcile tolerates a source unavailable (keeps the old snapshot); replace can't — a new baseline has no "old snapshot fallback," every source must be freshly observed on the spot.
  • Why block: if a source is "once present, now unavailable," forcing a new baseline silently erases it. opencode rather ReplacementBlocked, keeping the old epoch, awaiting retry, than deliver an incomplete baseline — the ultimate cash-out of Lesson 21's unavailable ≠ removed.
  • fence + revision: double-check agent/version before writing, mismatch → back off and retry — ensuring the switch is atomic, consistent, never a half-switched garbled epoch.
  • Two defenses (block against incompleteness + fence against garbling) point to one belief: an epoch is either established completely and consistently, or not touched — no half-baked middle state permitted.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why does SystemContext.replace block on "a source unavailable" when switching agents?
  1. Because agents can't be switched
  2. Because replace is too slow
  3. To save memory
  4. The new baseline rebuilds from scratch with no old-snapshot fallback; a source once present now unseen, forcing it through would silently erase it from the new agent's world
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. reconcile (same agent) tolerates a missing source — keep the old snapshot. But replace stitches a brand-new baseline with no fallback; every source must be freshly observed now. If a source is "once present, now unavailable," forcing it erases it, the new agent wrongly thinks "not a git repo." Rather block than deliver an incomplete baseline — the ultimate cash-out of Lesson 21's unavailable≠removed.
2. Why is the same "a source unread" trivial for reconcile but grave for replace?
  1. Because replace uses a different model
  2. There's no difference
  3. reconcile stands on an existing baseline (old snapshot as safety net); replace lays a foundation from scratch, missing a piece welds the gap into the bedrock
  4. Because reconcile doesn't read sources
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. An increment missing one entry catches up a round later, the baseline always complete; laying a foundation missing a piece welds the gap into the bedrock, carried every round after. "Laying a foundation" and "adding bricks" are fundamentally different — opencode treating them differently is exactly seeing through this.
3. What role do fence + revision play in agent switching?
  1. Log the switch
  2. Double-check agent/version before write, mismatch → back off and retry — ensuring an atomic, consistent switch, never a half-switched garbled epoch
  3. Speed up the switch
  4. Block all switches
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. fence rechecks before write: is the current agent still what I thought? is revision still what I read? Any mismatch = someone acted while I planned → die RevisionMismatch/AgentMismatch → outer retryRevisionMismatch backs off and retries. Optimistic concurrency ensures two concurrent switches settle just once, cleanly.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says opencode keeps two defenses: block against incompleteness, fence against garbling, both pointing to "an epoch is either established completely and consistently, or not touched." With the "reliable system vs script that runs" divide, explain why "gracefully breaking nothing in the abnormal case" is harder and more valuable than "running in the normal case."
  • Lesson 21's unavailable≠removed principle seemed like inconsequential fastidiousness then, yet six lessons later bears its most important fruit in the critical "switch agent" scenario. From code/systems you've read, give an example where "an early, unremarkable bit of restraint later became a key capability."

The LLM layer

Part 4's agent loop calls llm.stream(request) each round, consulting the model once. Part 6, we drill into what's behind that one call — how opencode deals with model providers that each speak their own tongue: Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Bedrock, Copilot… each API's dialect differs: how messages are arranged, how tools are declared, how streaming responses are chunked, all different. Yet opencode's core speaks only one language, start to finish. How? The answer is a whole translation system — this lesson builds the master diagram first: core speaks one canonical language, a layer of protocol adapters translates it into each dialect and translates each reply back. Grasp this diagram and you've caught all of Part 6's skeleton.

Why does "interfacing with many models" deserve a whole part? Because it's a badly-underestimated dirty, tiring job. Each provider's API evolves on its own: different field names, different tool-call protocols, different error formats, different streaming-chunk granularity, and revisions now and then. If you let core's business logic directly accommodate these differences, core gets riddled and corroded by each dialect's detail — ugly if (provider === "anthropic") ... else if ... branches everywhere. opencode's countermeasure is the classic "anti-corruption layer" idea: between core and external providers, build a translation wall; inside the wall is always clean canonical language, and the messiness outside is all blocked at the adapter layer. This lesson sees the wall's overall structure.

🌐 Analogy
Think of opencode's core as a commander who speaks only his native tongue, doing business with five contractors who each speak a different dialect (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini…). The dumbest way is making the commander learn five dialects himself — he'd be exhausted and led astray by these fiddly language details. The smart way gives him a team of simultaneous interpreters (protocol adapters): the commander always states needs in his native tongue (a canonical LLMRequest), the interpreter translates it into the right contractor's dialect and sends it; the contractor replies in dialect, the interpreter translates back to the native tongue (canonical LLMEvent) for the commander. The commander speaks only one language throughout, the five dialects' grunt work all digested at the interpreter layer. A sixth contractor shows up one day? Add one more interpreter, the commander changes not a word. This interpreter team is opencode's LLM protocol layer.

One canonical language: LLMRequest and LLMEvent

This translation system stands on core and adapters agreeing on a canonical "intermediate language." The outbound direction is LLMRequest — a unified request object expressing "what I want to ask" in a form independent of any provider:

system
system prompt (baseline + mid-conversation updates, Part 5)
messages
canonicalized conversation messages
tools / toolChoice
available tools and selection policy
generation
temperature, max tokens, and other generation params
providerOptions
the few genuinely provider-specific escape hatches

The inbound direction is a stream of LLMEvent — the model's response translated into a string of unified events: text chunks, reasoning chunks, tool calls, usage stats, errors… Remember Lesson 17's loop line llm.stream(request).pipe(Stream.runForEach(event => ...))? The event it consumes is exactly this canonical LLMEvent. Whether the underlying is Anthropic or Gemini, the loop sees identically-shaped events — exactly the translation wall's whole value: the agent loop inside the wall knows nothing of the provider, and needn't.

Pause on the weight of "one canonical language." Its essence is nailing down, between core and the outside world, a contract both sides honor: core promises "I'll always request in this shape, receive responses in this shape," and adapters promise "however messy outside, I'll tidy it into this shape." Once this contract stands, both sides can evolve independently: core wants a new tool-call style, just add it to the canon, no need to mind how a dozen providers implement it; a provider changes its API, just change that adapter, core unaware. A canonical language isn't a restriction but a decoupling freedom — it cleanly separates "how the business evolves" from "how providers evolve," two things that would otherwise drag each other down. You'll find this is the same wisdom as Lesson 12's "decouple front/back ends with an OpenAPI spec": stand a contract both machines/sides honor in the middle, and both ends gain freedom from being held hostage by the other.

core / agent loopspeaks only canonical
LLMRequest →
protocol adapterthe translation wall
each dialect →
some providerAnthropic/OpenAI…

Two layers: protocol vs provider

Inside the translation wall, opencode makes another cut, into two clear layers: protocol and provider. These two words are often conflated, but opencode separates them firmly — understanding this distinction is the key to this layer. An analogy: a protocol is like a plug standard (two-prong, three-prong, UK, EU), a provider like a specific appliance. One plug standard fits a whole pile of appliances, one appliance (dual-voltage) may support two standards — standard and appliance should be discussed separately.

protocol = a "wire format"

Defines exactly what the request/response look like on the wire: how fields are named, how tools are encoded, how the stream is chunked. opencode has 6: Anthropic Messages, OpenAI Chat, OpenAI Responses, OpenAI-Compatible, Gemini, Bedrock Converse.

provider = a "specific vendor"

A service you can actually connect to: which protocol + how to authenticate + which endpoint. Like anthropic, openai, google, bedrock, azure, github-copilot, openrouter, xai…

This cut is masterful because protocol and provider are "many-to-many." One protocol can serve many providers: the OpenAI Chat protocol isn't used only by OpenAI itself but by a whole crowd of "OpenAI-compatible" vendors (OpenRouter, xAI, various local models…) speaking through it. Conversely, one provider may support several protocols: OpenAI itself has both the old Chat and the new Responses. Decoupling "wire format" (protocol) from "specific vendor" (provider) lets opencode cover a dozen-plus providers reusing just 6 protocols — not writing a fresh set per vendor. Few protocols, much reuse — that's where this layering pays off most.

Why not just "one adapter per provider" and skip the "protocol" abstraction? Because that breeds much duplication. OpenRouter, xAI, local Ollama — their API forms are nearly identical to OpenAI's; per-provider sets would copy the same "OpenAI dialect translation" three or four times, and a change needs changing in three or four places. opencode extracts the "dialect" into a standalone "protocol," precisely seeing that same-form providers essentially share one wire format. So the provider layer becomes ultra-thin — almost just "which protocol I use, how to authenticate, which endpoint," while the truly laborious "translation" logic settles into the few protocols for reuse. Sink the duplicated parts down, lift the volatile parts up — the eternal motif of layered design, here again.

What the translation wall blocks

To feel the wall's value, imagine without it. If the agent loop directly called each vendor's SDK, then "send a message with a tool call" would have to be written as a tangle in core: Anthropic's tools go in a tools array with input_schema; OpenAI's in functions (or the new tools) with parameters; Gemini is yet another functionDeclarations… every "call the model" in core would sprout a vine of if (provider === ...) branches, choked in each dialect's detail. Worse, when a vendor revises, you'd hunt and change all over core.

❌ no translation wall

core directly interfaces each SDK. Every "call the model" if-elses by provider; each dialect's detail corrodes business logic; a vendor revision means changing all over core; a new provider means touching core.

✅ with translation wall

core speaks only canonical. Provider differences all locked at the adapter layer; a vendor revision changes only its own protocol; a new provider = add one adapter, core unchanged.

This is exactly why an "anti-corruption layer" is so named: it guards against the outside world's chaos and volatility corroding into your clean core. The agent loop inside the wall forever lives in a simple world of "one model, one request, one event" — that simplicity isn't because reality is simple but because an adapter layer carries off all of reality's complexity for it. The reason you were never bothered by "which model is this" reading Part 4 is exactly this wall quietly working.

1agent loop: llm.stream(canonical LLMRequest)
2routing picks a provider → that provider's designated protocol
3protocol.encode: canonical request → this vendor's dialect JSON, sent
4provider streams back (this vendor's dialect chunks)
5protocol.decode: dialect chunks → canonical LLMEvent stream, back to the loop

Few protocols, covering many providers

Nail the "protocol vs provider" relationship with concrete numbers. opencode maintains 6 protocols in all, yet covers a dozen-plus providers with them:

Anthropic Messages
serves Anthropic (Claude)
OpenAI Chat / Responses
serves OpenAI itself (old and new)
OpenAI-Compatible
serves a crowd of compatible vendors: OpenRouter, xAI, local models…
Gemini
serves Google Gemini
Bedrock Converse
serves various models on AWS Bedrock

See the leverage? The OpenAI-Compatible protocol single-handedly catches "every vendor on the market claiming OpenAI compatibility" — their number keeps growing, yet opencode adds not a line of new protocol code. That's the compound interest of "abstracting the wire format into a protocol": "OpenAI-compatible" has become a de facto industry standard, and opencode, implementing this standard once, free-rides the whole ecosystem. Fewest protocols, most providers leveraged — this layer's economy is plain in this number contrast. It also explains why Lesson 31 spends a whole lesson on the OpenAI protocol: it serves not one vendor but half an ecosystem.

🗺️ The big picture · Part 6 prologue

This lesson sets the outline for Part 6. The whole LLM layer unfolds over the next lessons:

  • Lesson 28 · this one: the master diagram — core speaks canonical (LLMRequest/LLMEvent), protocol adapters as the "translation wall"; protocol vs provider two layers.
  • Lesson 29: a protocol adapter's common skeleton — what "translation" duties a protocol must implement.
  • Lessons 30-32: the three big dialects in detail — Anthropic (30), OpenAI Chat/Responses (31), Gemini & Bedrock (32).
  • Lessons 33-35: routing & transport (33), streaming & caching (34), model resolution & Copilot (35).

Remember this lesson's core intuition: opencode's core forever speaks only one canonical language, fully isolating the dirty job of "interfacing with wildly different providers" into a protocol-adapter layer. This is a textbook practice of "anti-corruption layer / adapter pattern" on the "multi-model interfacing" dimension. Next lesson we approach the translation wall to see what "translation duties" make up a single protocol adapter inside.

🔬 Source detail

Core outwardly uses only a few entries exposed by the standalone package @opencode-ai/llm, quite clean:

// packages/llm/src/llm.ts — the LLM interface core sees
export const stream = LLMClient.stream      // streaming: emits an LLMEvent stream (used in Lesson 17)
export const generate = LLMClient.generate  // one-shot
export const request = (input) => new LLMRequest({ ... })  // canonical request

Note the LLM layer is a standalone package (packages/llm), not inside packages/core. This physical split is itself a concrete boundary of the "translation wall": all the detail of protocols, providers, each dialect is locked inside this package; core only imports the few clean entries it exposes — LLM.stream/request etc. Locking the dirty job in a standalone package even makes the dependency graph declare "who should know dialects, who shouldn't." This is "separation of concerns" carried thoroughly to the package level. A simple rule of thumb follows: any "how this provider behaves" knowledge should live only in this package; once it leaks into core, that's a broken window to watch.

🎯 Key points
  • Behind Lesson 17's llm.stream(request) is a whole translation system: core speaks one canonical language, protocol adapters translate to each dialect and back.
  • Canonical intermediate language: outbound is a unified LLMRequest (system/messages/tools/generation/providerOptions), inbound a unified LLMEvent stream — the agent loop knows nothing of the provider.
  • Anti-corruption layer idea: isolate the dirty job of "accommodating wildly different providers" at the protocol-adapter layer, banning if (provider === …) corrosion all over core.
  • Protocol vs provider, two layers: protocol = wire format (6: Anthropic/OpenAI Chat/OpenAI Responses/OpenAI-Compatible/Gemini/Bedrock); provider = specific vendor (which protocol + auth + endpoint). Many-to-many, few protocols, much reuse.
  • The LLM layer is a standalone package @opencode-ai/llm, physically isolating the dirty job; core imports only a few clean entries.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. How does opencode's core deal with model providers that each speak their own tongue?
  1. Core speaks only one canonical language (LLMRequest/LLMEvent); a protocol-adapter layer translates to each dialect and back
  2. Lets the model adapt itself
  3. Supports only one provider
  4. core handles each with if (provider === ...) everywhere
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Classic anti-corruption layer: a translation wall between core and external providers. Core always speaks canonical (outbound LLMRequest, inbound LLMEvent stream), adapters translate to each dialect and replies back to canonical events. The agent loop inside knows nothing of the provider — Lesson 17's llm.stream consumes exactly these canonical LLMEvents.
2. What's the difference between a "protocol" and a "provider"?
  1. Protocol = wire format (what request/response look like, 6 of them); provider = specific vendor (which protocol + auth + endpoint); many-to-many
  2. They are identical
  3. Protocols are new, providers are old
  4. Protocols are paid, providers are free
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Protocol is like a plug standard, provider like a specific appliance: one standard fits many appliances, one appliance may support two standards. The OpenAI Chat protocol isn't used only by OpenAI but by many "OpenAI-compatible" vendors; OpenAI itself has both Chat and Responses. Decoupling → 6 protocols reusably cover a dozen-plus providers.
3. Why is the OpenAI-Compatible protocol especially high-leverage?
  1. Because it's cheapest
  2. Because it runs fastest
  3. "OpenAI-compatible" is a de facto standard; implementing it once free-rides the whole ecosystem (OpenRouter/xAI/local models…), new vendors needing no new code
  4. Because it's OpenAI official
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. A crowd of vendors claim "OpenAI compatibility" with near-identical API forms. Abstracting this wire format into one protocol, opencode implements it once and all of them auto-connect — their number grows while opencode adds not a line of new protocol code. That's the compound interest of abstracting the wire format into a protocol.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson calls "one canonical language" the same wisdom as Lesson 12's "decouple front/back ends with an OpenAPI spec": stand a contract both sides honor in the middle, both ends gain freedom from being held hostage. From an integration you've done, give an example where "a middle contract letting both ends evolve" benefited you (or its absence hurt).
  • The lesson gives a rule of thumb: "any 'how this provider behaves' knowledge should live only in the llm package; once it leaks into core, that's a broken window." Why is controlling the physical location of such knowledge more reliable than a verbal agreement "let's not write provider special-cases in core"?

Protocol adapters

Last lesson drew the big picture of the "translation wall": core speaks the canonical language, protocol adapters translate it into each vendor's dialect. This lesson walks up to that wall and looks at what a single protocol adapter is made of, internally. The good news: opencode abstracts this with extraordinary cleanliness—every protocol, whether it translates Anthropic's or Gemini's dialect, fills in the same two-column form. One column handles outbound (how to encode a canonical request into this vendor's body), one handles inbound (how to decode this vendor's streaming response back into canonical events). Once you grasp the shape of this form, reading any specific protocol in lessons 30–32 becomes just watching "the same form, filled in with a different dialect."

Why look at the "skeleton" first, then specific protocols? Because commonality matters more than difference. Six protocols look wildly varied, but the problem they solve is isomorphic: send one unified request out, take a stream of responses back. opencode nails this "isomorphic problem" into one Protocol interface—a form every protocol must fill. Learn the cells of this form, and you have a cross-reading framework: for any vendor, you can instantly locate "where its request encoding lives, where its stream decoding lives," instead of drowning in one dialect's details. This lesson gives you the map for the next three.

📋 Analogy
Each interpreter in last lesson's relay actually holds an identical "job description" (the Protocol interface). It mandates only two hard duties: One, "outbound translation"—take the chief's request, phrased in the mother tongue, and assemble one complete document the matching contractor can read (the request body). Two, "inbound translation"—and this one's subtle: the contractor's reply doesn't come all at once, it arrives word by word, in halting fragments; the interpreter must listen-and-jot, holding context, piecing that stream of fragments back into complete mother-tongue sentences (streaming decode). Whatever dialect you handle, the "shape" of these two duties is identical—only the specific knack of "how you assemble the document" and "how you piece fragments" differs per dialect. One unified job description, six dialects' different fillings—that's the skeleton of a protocol adapter.

One two-column form: body and stream

Open route/protocol.ts and the Protocol interface is clean as a table—just two big blocks, matching exactly "out" and "back":

// simplified from route/protocol.ts
interface Protocol<Body, Frame, Event, State> {
  id: ProtocolID
  body: {                          // ← request side (out)
    schema: Codec<Body>            //   shape of this vendor's body
    from: (LLMRequest) => Body     //   canonical request → this body
  }
  stream: {                        // ← response side (back): a state machine
    event: Codec<Event, Frame>     //   one wire frame → one decoded event
    initial: (LLMRequest) => State //   initial parser state
    step: (State, Event) => [State, LLMEvent[]]  // one event → canonical events + new state
  }
}

The whole interface is two blocks: body owns the request, stream owns the response. The split itself is a one-line design philosophy: all a protocol does is the round trip of "translate canonical request into dialect, translate dialect response back into canonical." body.from is the entirety of "outbound translation"—one function, eats an LLMRequest, emits a body this vendor understands. stream is "inbound translation," far more involved—a state machine (next section). One form, two columns; fill both, and a protocol is born.

One more detail worth noting: those four type parameters in the header, <Body, Frame, Event, State>, actually force each protocol to answer four questions—what does your request body look like (Body)? what's one raw frame on the wire (Frame)? what's the structure of one decoded event (Event)? what state must your parser accumulate (State)? Once a protocol fills these four "unknowns" with concrete types, TypeScript can infer all the types down the schemas and functions—the source comment notes you usually need not write type arguments when assembling a protocol; the schemas and parser functions are themselves the source of truth. In other words, this form isn't just a "documentary" convention—it's a typed mold: fill the four blanks and the compiler guarantees "request body shape," "event decode," and "state flow" all line up end to end.

body · request side (out)

A from(LLMRequest) function, encoding the canonical request into this vendor's body. Simple, formed in one shot.

stream · response side (back)

A state machine, decoding this vendor's streaming response frame by frame, accumulating, translating back into canonical LLMEvent.

Drawn in three dimensions, this form is the three layers below—outermost is the protocol's identity and two duties, one layer in is which parts each duty is made of. You'll notice all the complexity is loaded onto the stream column: the request side is two light parts, the response side hangs a five-part machine. That visual imbalance, "light on one side, heavy on the other," is itself a reminder: when reading any protocol, the real work is in stream.

Protocol
id (protocol identity) + two duties: body / stream
body (request side)
schema (body shape) + from (canonical request → this body)
stream (response side)
event + initial + step + terminal? + onHalt? (a state machine)

Outbound: body.from, a one-function affair

Take the light column first. body.from's signature is plain to the extreme: (request: LLMRequest) => Effect<Body>—feed in a canonical request, get out a body this vendor recognizes. Why can it be this simple? Because the request is static: the moment you hit send, what you want to say, which messages you carry, which tool definitions you give, how many tokens you want—are all already fixed. No "thinking while talking," no "saying half then amending." So "outbound translation" is a pure data transformation: take the fields of the canonical structure, lay them out again under this dialect's field names and nesting.

LLMRequest
canonical request
body.from →
rename / rearrange fields
messages, tools, temperature… per dialect
→
Body
this vendor's body

So what's schema for? It's the shape contract of this vendor's body—a Codec<Body>. It carries two values: one, it gives from's output a checkable target type (the encoded body must look like this, errors caught at the type level); two, at actual send time, it encodes the Body into a JSON string (recall lesson 28's protocol.body.schema encode). from handles "is the content right," schema handles "is the shape right"; together the request side is complete. This column lifts heavy things lightly precisely because it faces a request that has "already settled"—the hard part was never translating a finished draft outward, but piecing a still-flowing utterance back together as you listen.

Why request is simple, response is a state machine

What's most intriguing about this form is its asymmetry: the request side is just a function, the response side is a state machine. This asymmetry isn't arbitrary—it faithfully maps reality. A request you assemble all at once and send whole—so one from function suffices. But a response the model emits streaming: text arrives token by token, one tool call's argument JSON arrives across several chunks, usage stats appear only at the end… you can't wait for it all to arrive before processing (that defeats the whole point of "streaming"), you must decode as you receive. And "decode as you receive" inherently needs to remember "where you are so far"—which is exactly why the state machine exists.

initialeach response start, build an initial parser state State
eventreceive a wire frame Frame → decode into one Event
stepstep(State, Event) → [new State, emitted LLMEvents]
…loop…each frame steps once, state advances, events emit
onHaltstream ends → optionally flush the last batch of LLMEvents

The signature (State, Event) => [State, LLMEvent[]] is the soul of the whole response side, and a classic shape of state transition in functional style: give me "current state" and "one newly-arrived event," I return "next state" and "the canonical events this step should emit." Take a tool call: the first frame step might only note "start accumulating a tool call named read" (emits nothing); later frames piece the argument JSON into State chunk by chunk (still emit nothing); once the arguments are complete, the final frame emits one whole toolCall canonical event. Fragments in, whole out—powered by State quietly accumulating in the middle. This state machine is the machine that reassembles "the dialect's streaming fragments" into "canonical complete events."

Lay that tool-call example out frame by frame and the machine's "accumulate" gets concrete—note the first three steps emit empty canonical events while State quietly thickens; only once arguments are complete does it "pop" out one whole event:

frame1"begin tool call read" → State notes {name:read, args:''}, emits []
frame2arg chunk '{\"path\":' → State.args appended, emits []
frame3arg chunk '\"a.ts\"}' → State.args appended, JSON now complete
frame4"tool call done" → emits whole LLMEvent.toolCall{name:read, args:{path:'a.ts'}}

That's the entire meaning of State: it's the "scratch paper" that buffers cross-frame fragments until they're whole. Without it, receiving the half-JSON "{\"path\":" leaves you nothing to do—it's neither valid JSON nor any complete meaning. With State, each frame does one small thing (append, note); completeness judgment and event emission wait for the "all assembled" moment. This also explains why step returns the "new state" alongside: the machine isn't allowed to secretly mutate globals, every step explicitly hands the scratch paper to the next, so the whole decode chain becomes a string of pure, predictable transforms. Lay the response side's five parts side by side and each one's job is plain:

event
decode one wire frame Frame into one structured Event (Codec)
initial
each response start, build an initial parser State from the request
step
core: eats (State, Event), emits [new State, some LLMEvents]
terminal?
optional: judge whether an event marks "request done" (for transports that don't end naturally)
onHalt?
optional: at stream end, flush the last batch of buffered LLMEvents
🗺️ The Big Picture

This lesson gives the skeleton all protocols share, the map for the next three lessons:

  • One Protocol = a two-column form: body (request side) + stream (response side). Every protocol fills this form.
  • body.from: one function, canonical LLMRequest → this vendor's body. Outbound translation, formed in one shot.
  • stream is a state machine: initial (build state) → step(State, Event) (fragments in, accumulate, emit canonical events) → onHalt (wrap up).
  • asymmetry maps reality: request sent all at once (a function suffices), response arrives streaming (must be a state machine, decode as you receive).

With this form, the next three lessons get clear: each is just filling the same form in a different dialect. Lesson 30 watches Anthropic fill it (what's special about its tool blocks, cache breakpoints); lesson 31 the OpenAI Chat and Responses fillings; lesson 32 Gemini and Bedrock. Read them carrying one question: "how is this vendor's body.from and stream.step special, versus the others?"—you already grasp the skeleton, the rest is all dialect flavor.

🔬 Source Detail

How is a protocol "assembled"? Look at the last lines of anthropic-messages.ts—it's filling exactly this form:

// simplified from end of protocols/anthropic-messages.ts
export const protocol = Protocol.make({
  id: ADAPTER,
  body: { schema: ..., from: ... },        // request side
  stream: { event: ..., initial: ..., step: ... },  // response side
})

The intriguing part is Protocol.make itself—its implementation is (input) => input, an identity function! It does nothing at runtime, purely a "typed seam": the source comment is explicit, "the schemas and parser functions are the source of truth," and make exists to reserve a unified entry point for future cross-cutting concerns (like adding tracing, instrumentation). It's a restrained foresight: nothing extra is needed at assembly today, but leaving a make shell means when you later want to add one layer across all protocols, there's a single place to do it, without touching six protocol definitions. An identity function that does nothing today is a door left open for tomorrow's "unified retrofit."

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • Every protocol adapter fills the same two-column form (the Protocol interface, route/protocol.ts): body (request side) + stream (response side).
  • body.from: one function, encoding the canonical LLMRequest into this vendor's request body—outbound translation, formed in one shot.
  • stream is a state machine: initial builds state → step(State, Event) => [State, LLMEvent[]] accumulates and reassembles dialect streaming fragments into canonical events → onHalt wraps up.
  • asymmetry maps reality: a request is sent all at once (a function suffices), a response arrives streaming and needs cross-frame accumulation (e.g. tool-call arguments arriving in chunks), so it must be a state machine decoding as it receives.
  • Protocol.make is an identity function, a pure typed seam—reserving a unified entry for future cross-cutting concerns (tracing etc.). This form is the map for lessons 30–32.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What does the Protocol interface (route/protocol.ts) look like?
  1. A two-column form: body (request side, encode canonical request into this body) + stream (response side, a state machine decoding the streaming response back into canonical events)
  2. A pile of if (provider === ...) branches
  3. Just a single send() method
  4. One giant function with all providers' logic together
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. The interface is clean as a table, just two blocks: body owns "out," stream owns "back." body is one from(LLMRequest)→Body function plus a schema; stream is a five-part state machine (initial/event/step/terminal?/onHalt?). Every protocol fills this same form — grasp it and lessons 30–32 are just "the same form filled in different dialects."
2. Why is the request side just a function, but the response side a state machine?
  1. A request is static, assembled and sent whole at once; a response arrives as streaming fragments (e.g. tool-arg JSON across frames), so you must accumulate as you receive, remembering "where you are so far"
  2. Pure legacy baggage, no reason
  3. Because responses are bigger than requests
  4. To make the code longer
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. The asymmetry faithfully maps reality. A request is settled the moment you hit send — one from function suffices. But a response streams back: one tool call's argument JSON arrives across several frames — receiving the half "{\"path\":" leaves you nothing to do, you must use State as scratch paper, accumulate, and emit a whole event only once assembled. step(State,Event)→[State, LLMEvent[]] exists exactly for this.
3. Protocol.make is implemented as (input) => input, an identity function. Why does it exist?
  1. It validates and fixes the request body
  2. As a "typed seam": doing nothing today, but reserving a single entry point to later add cross-cutting concerns (like tracing) across all protocols at once
  3. It's a bug and should be deleted
  4. It sends the HTTP request
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The source comment is explicit: the schemas and parser functions are the source of truth, make does nothing at runtime. But leaving this shell is restrained foresight — when you later want to add one layer (tracing, instrumentation) across six protocols, there's a single place to do it, without touching each protocol's definition. An identity function doing nothing today is a door left open for tomorrow's unified retrofit.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • stream.step's signature is (State, Event) => [State, LLMEvent[]] — it returns the "new state" alongside, rather than secretly mutating a global. Using this lesson's tool-call example (arguments arriving across frames), discuss what "explicitly threading state" buys this streaming-decode state machine over "in-place mutation" (hint: predictability, testability, concurrency).
  • This lesson says the six protocols solve an "isomorphic problem," so it nails them into one Protocol interface every protocol fills. Recall a "many backends/formats but essentially isomorphic" scenario you've met (e.g. multiple payment channels, multiple export formats). Had you abstracted such a "unified interface form," how much easier would adding a new channel/format become? Conversely, what happens without it?

The Anthropic protocol

Last lesson we learned the blank "two-column form" (the Protocol interface) inside out: body owns out, stream owns back. This lesson we look at the first form actually filled in—the Anthropic Messages protocol. It's opencode's "primary dialect" (the Claude family rides this), and the one that fills the form with the most personality. Reading it isn't just learning one protocol—it's the first time we see "the skeleton grow flesh." Anthropic's filling has two signature quirks worth remembering forever: one, "everything is a block"—request and response content are both split into typed "content blocks"; two, "a budget of four cache breakpoints"—a constraint so neat it's almost harsh, which forces out a rather beautiful piece of budget-management code.

Why lead with Anthropic? Because its design best captures the weight of "protocol = dialect." The same "send a conversation, take a reply," yet Anthropic insists you pack content into labeled boxes, insists the system prompt be hoisted into its own top-level field, and insists on a hard cache cap that 400s if exceeded. These "insists" are the dialect's accent. And what opencode's body.from (here a family of lower* functions) does is translate core's accent-free canonical request, word by word, into Anthropic-accented speech. Understand this translation and you understand the full weight of the word "adapter."

📦 Analogy
Imagine, on behalf of the chief, you fill out a customs form for an especially rule-bound contractor (Anthropic). This one has two dead rules. Rule one: everything goes into "labeled standard boxes"—text one box (text), image one box (image), "please run this tool" one box (tool_use), "here's the tool's result" another box (tool_result). No loose packing, no mixed packing, even "system notice" must go alone into the form's header field, not mixed in with cargo. Rule two is even sharper: you get only 4 "priority cache" stickers total. To make the contractor "remember, don't recompute" certain boxes, you stick one; but there are only 4 stickers, and sticking a 5th gets the whole form bounced on the spot (a 400 error). So you must budget carefully: count stickers as you pack, and once 4 are used, anything else you'd have stuck you can only quietly note "wanted to but didn't," then give up. These two dead rules—standard boxes, sticker budget—are exactly the sharpest accent of this Anthropic protocol.

Everything is a "block": typed content boxes

Open protocols/anthropic-messages.ts and the first thing hitting you is a long run of "block" types defined by Schema.tag(...). Unlike some protocols that stuff message content into one big string, Anthropic requires every piece of content be a structured block with a type tag:

text
plain text block
image
image block (multimodal input)
tool_use
assistant wants to call a tool (with id, name, input)
tool_result
returning a tool result (with matching tool_use_id)
server_tool_use
server-side tool (e.g. web search, run by the vendor itself)
*_tool_result
server tool results (web_search/code_execution/web_fetch, inlined whole)

This "everything is a block" design buys clear structure, validatability, and metadata-carrying—each block can hang its own cache_control (the star of the next section). There's also an interesting asymmetry hidden here: a regular tool_use (client tool) is a three-step round trip of "assistant asks, opencode runs it locally, sends back a tool_result"; but those few server-side tools (web_search / code_execution / web_fetch) are run by the vendor itself, with results inlined whole into the assistant turn—the source comment notes they "arrive whole, with no streaming deltas," with no client-side tool_result round trip. A "block"'s type tag actually encodes an execution-semantics difference of "who runs this tool," showing how refined this block-type design is. Another easily-missed detail: the system prompt in Anthropic is not a message but a separate top-level field of the request body, typed as "an array of text blocks."
