// And a tool turn reuses many times → sure win. So on by default.

A cache write costs slightly more than no cache (1.25x), but a read is nearly free (0.1x). As long as the prefix is reused even once within 5 minutes, the deal pays off; and a tool turn easily has a dozen-plus round-trips, reusing far more than once—so opencode sets caching on by default, matching the consensus of production agent frameworks like LangChain's caching middleware and kern-ai. One more engineering detail worth noting: markMessageAt deliberately avoids .map() and uses slice + index assignment, the comment frank about it—long conversations run it on every request, and .map()'s closure dispatch and whole-array copies "show up in profiling." Even such tiny hot-path overhead is watched—the hallmark of production code, and the confidence behind this cache logic daring to be "on by default."

Different vendors, different cache mechanisms: a restrained "skip"

One last touch of cleverness hides in a line, RESPECTS_INLINE_HINTS. cache-policy's whole "mark inline hints on parts" approach works only for Anthropic and Bedrock—because only their wire formats recognize the inline cache_control marker. OpenAI's and Gemini's cache mechanisms are fundamentally different:

VendorCache mechanismWhat cache-policy does
Anthropic / Bedrockexplicit inline marker cache_controlplace breakpoints (this lesson's star)
OpenAIimplicit prefix caching (automatic, no marker)skip the whole pass
Geminiimplicit + out-of-band CachedContent APIskip the whole pass

So applyCachePolicy's very first line is: anything not in RESPECTS_INLINE_HINTS returns as-is, the whole policy skipped. The comment explains candidly: marking inline hints for OpenAI/Gemini is "harmless but pointless"—they don't look at these markers at all, having their own implicit caching. It's a very restrained design: not forcing one mechanism on everyone, but acknowledging each vendor's fundamentally different caching philosophy, acting when it should (Anthropic/Bedrock) and shutting up when it should (OpenAI/Gemini). This again confirms all of M6's main melody—let the adapter layer absorb differences, rather than cramming them into one unified abstraction pretending they're all the same. A truly good abstraction knows where to unify and where to let go.

Drawn as a pipeline, applyCachePolicy's "fill-in-the-blanks" nature is clear—it fills only the cells the caller left empty, never overwriting existing manual markers:

LLMRequest
incoming
recognize route?→
non-inline family
OpenAI/Gemini → return as-is
yes → resolve(auto)
place 3 breakpoints
last tool/system/user
→ hand to protocol
L30 breakpoint budget
trim within 4 slots

Note this pipeline's handoff to lesson 30: cache-policy runs before protocol encoding, marking the parts to cache with CacheHint; then the protocol's lowering layer (lesson 30's cacheControl with the Breakpoints{remaining,dropped} counter) turns these hints into actual cache_control markers while holding the 4-slot cap. One decides "where," one executes "mark it without exceeding"—responsibilities cleanly split: cache-policy is strategy (which spots are most worth caching), the protocol's breakpoint budget is tactics (carrying it out within the hard constraint). The two lessons now mesh seamlessly.

🗺️ The Big Picture

This lesson ties M6's (and earlier parts') several threads into knots:

  • LLMEvent = the inbound half of the anti-corruption layer (schema/events.ts, 16 members): step / text / reasoning / tool-input start-delta-end triples + tool-call/result/error + finish(with Usage) + provider-error. All six protocols' stream.step translate into it, lesson 17's agent loop consumes only it. Completes the inbound face of lesson 28's "translation wall."
  • Auto caching's 3 breakpoints (cache-policy.ts's "auto"): last tool definition + last system part + latest user message—caching the "stable prefix unchanged through the whole turn."
  • Connecting lesson 24: breakpoints at the "latest user message" boundary make every assistant↔tool round-trip within a turn hit the same cached prefix; this is the true purpose of Context Epoch guarding "baseline prefix stable"—stable = cacheable = money saved.
  • Caching on by default: write 1.25x, read 0.1x, one reuse within 5 minutes pays off, a tool turn reuses many times for a sure win. Mechanisms differ (Anthropic/Bedrock explicit inline; OpenAI/Gemini implicit, cache-policy skips the whole pass).

M6 now has just one lesson left. Lesson 35 covers model resolution and the GitHub Copilot provider: how opencode recognizes from the models.dev catalog "which Route a model should ride, which pieces to configure," and Copilot, that "both a provider and a self-contained auth system" special case. After it, the whole LLM loop—"core speaks canonical → adapters translate dialects → transport delivers → event stream comes back → caching saves money"—is fully closed.

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • LLMEvent (schema/events.ts, 16 members) is the anti-corruption layer's inbound canonical vocabulary: step-start/finish, text/reasoning/tool-input each as start-delta-end triples, tool-call/result/error, finish (with Usage), provider-error. All six protocols' stream.step translate into it, the agent loop (L17) consumes only it—completing the inbound half of lesson 28's "translation wall."
  • The triple reflects streaming: content arriving "bit by bit" (body/reasoning/tool args) all split into start→delta…→end, exactly lesson 29's state-machine output shape.
  • cache-policy.ts's "auto" places 3 breakpoints: last tool definition + last system part + latest user message (markLastTool/markLastSystem/markMessages), caching the "stable prefix unchanged through the turn."
  • Connects Context Epoch (L24): breakpoints at the "latest user message" boundary make every assistant↔tool round-trip in a turn hit the same prefix; so Context Epoch guarding "baseline prefix stable" is truly to preserve cache hits. Caching on by default (write 1.25x/read 0.1x, one reuse within 5 min pays off).
  • RESPECTS_INLINE_HINTS = {anthropic-messages, bedrock-converse}: only these two recognize inline markers; OpenAI (implicit prefix caching), Gemini (implicit + out-of-band CachedContent) skip the whole pass. Embodies "let the adapter layer absorb differences, not pretend uniformity."

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What role does LLMEvent (schema/events.ts, 16 members) play in the whole design?
  1. A frontend data structure for UI rendering
  2. A DB table storing event logs
  3. The anti-corruption layer's "inbound" canonical vocabulary — all six protocols' stream.step translate their dialect into it, the agent loop consumes only it, completing the inbound face of lesson 28's "translation wall"
  4. Just Anthropic's internal event type
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Lesson 28's anti-corruption layer has an out and a back: out LLMRequest, back the LLMEvent stream. LLMEvent is the six protocols' "greatest common divisor" — Anthropic's content_block_delta, OpenAI's choices[].delta, Gemini's parts, Bedrock's binary frames, all translated by stream.step into this same 16-event set. So the agent loop (L17) drives any model knowing only these 16, and adding a seventh provider changes not a line of it.
2. cache-policy's "auto" default places breakpoints at "last tool definition, last system part, latest user message." Why these three?
  1. Up to these three is exactly the "stable prefix unchanged through the turn"; a turn explodes into many tool round-trips each resending that prefix, so marking the "latest user message" boundary makes every round-trip in the turn hit the cache
  2. Three randomly chosen positions
  3. Because these three are the shortest
  4. To fill the 4-breakpoint cap
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Tool definitions and system prompts usually stay fixed all turn, as does the prefix up to the "latest user message." In lesson 17's agent loop, one user message explodes into many assistant↔tool round-trips, each resending the whole prefix. Marking this boundary makes every round-trip's API call in the turn hit the same cached prefix — one write, N hits, multiplied savings. This is the true purpose of lesson 24's Context Epoch guarding "baseline prefix stable."
3. applyCachePolicy's first line checks RESPECTS_INLINE_HINTS, skipping the whole policy for OpenAI/Gemini and returning as-is. Why?
  1. It's a bug
  2. Because only Anthropic/Bedrock recognize inline cache_control markers; OpenAI uses implicit prefix caching, Gemini implicit + out-of-band CachedContent, so inline marks for them are "harmless but pointless"
  3. Because OpenAI/Gemini requests are too big
  4. Because OpenAI/Gemini don't support caching
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Cache mechanisms differ fundamentally: Anthropic/Bedrock want explicit inline cache_control marks on parts; OpenAI is implicit prefix caching (automatic, no marking); Gemini is implicit + out-of-band CachedContent API. cache-policy's "inline marking" matters only for the first two, harmless but pointless for the latter, so it skips entirely. A restrained design — acknowledging each vendor's caching philosophy rather than cramming into a fake-unified abstraction, exactly M6's main melody.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says LLMEvent splits "content arriving bit by bit" (body/reasoning/tool args) into a start→delta…→end triple. Consider: why does streaming UI (display-while-generating) almost necessarily require events shaped as "a head, continuous deltas, a tail"? If there were only one "complete event" and no deltas, what would the streaming experience lose? Where else have you seen this triple appear?
  • The lesson reveals a cross-lesson "conspiracy": cache-policy marks breakpoints at the stable-prefix boundary on the way out (strategy: where caching pays most), the protocol's breakpoint budget enforces it lower down within the 4-slot cap (tactics: executing within the hard constraint), while Context Epoch (L24) guards the prefix unchanged higher up (preserving hits). In your own words, articulate each of these three layers' responsibilities, and argue: why is splitting "decide where to cache," "execute marking," and "keep the prefix stable" across three different layers better than mashing them into one?

Model resolution & Copilot

This is M6's finale. Over the prior six lessons, we walked from the "anti-corruption layer" overview (L28) through the protocol skeleton (L29), four dialects (L30–32), transport's building blocks (L33), event stream and caching (L34). But one plain question lingered unanswered: when a user types a model name in the UI (say anthropic/claude-sonnet), how does opencode resolve that string all the way to "which Route to ride, which pieces to set, what price, what limits"? This lesson fills in that "from a name to a configured pipeline" resolution thread. And taking the final bow is GitHub Copilot—an "ultimate sample" that collects all six prior lessons' threads: it reuses OpenAI's protocol (even its very Route) yet brings its own distinctive auth, the last and most beautiful display of the "building-block architecture"'s power.

This lesson clarifies two things. One, opencode doesn't hardcode model facts in code, but fetches models.dev, a community-maintained "model encyclopedia"—whether a model supports tool calling, how big its context is, the price of input/output, the discount on cache read/write… all from this external catalog. Two, catalog turns this directory into a queryable in-memory structure, enabling the "model name → a configured Route" resolution. Understand these two and the whole LLM loop—"core speaks canonical → adapters translate dialects → transport delivers → events come back → caching saves money"—is fully closed.

📖 Analogy
Imagine opencode is a "universal translation company." Over six lessons we hired the interpreters (protocols) and arranged logistics (transport). But a client walks in saying just: "I want the expert 'Claude Sonnet.'" How does the company instantly pull up this person's complete résumé card from that name: what skills they have (capabilities), their fees (cost), the longest manuscript they take at once (context limit), which logistics line they use (Route)? Via a constantly-updated industry yellow pages (models.dev)—the company doesn't memorize each expert's data, but periodically syncs from this public directory, so a new expert is auto-queryable the moment they come online. And Copilot, this "expert," is especially special: the work he does is identical to OpenAI's (same protocol), but entering his door requires a special access pass—first take your GitHub badge to exchange for a temporary same-day pass, then enter with that pass. The same translation skill, wrapped in a distinctive access system—that's what sets Copilot apart.

models.dev: a "model encyclopedia" not hardcoded in code

The first key design: opencode hardcodes no model's facts in source. Information like "claude-sonnet supports 200K-token context," "gpt costs how much per million input tokens"—these keep changing with vendor releases; hardcoded in code, every model update would mean changing code and shipping a release, agonizing. opencode's solution is to fetch models.dev: a community-maintained public model catalog covering nearly all LLMs. The Model schema in models-dev.ts defines each model's tracked facts:

capabilities
attachment / reasoning / temperature / tool_call: supports attachments, reasoning, temperature, tools?
cost
unit prices for input/output/cache-read/cache-write (the very data lesson 34's reckoning used!)
limit
context cap, input/output token caps
modalities
input/output supported modalities: text/audio/image/video/pdf
family / release_date / status
model family, release date, alpha/beta/deprecated status

See those "cache read / cache write" prices in cost? Another connected thread—lesson 34's "write 1.25x, read 0.1x, one reuse pays off" reckoning was computed against exactly these prices from models.dev. The model encyclopedia isn't just an "address book," it's the factual bedrock for caching decisions and usage billing. models-dev.ts fetches this catalog over HTTP, caches it, and broadcasts a models-dev.refreshed event on refresh:

models.dev
community public catalog
HTTP fetch →
local cache
parsed into Model/Provider
on refresh →
broadcast refreshed
models-dev.refreshed event

Externalizing volatile facts into a self-updating directory means a new model coming online is often recognized by opencode without a line of code changed—the same "keep change out of the code" wisdom as lesson 31's "OpenAI-compatible free-rides the ecosystem," only that time it blocked "protocol changes," this time "model-fact changes." Deeper still, this is a transfer of responsibility: a model's prices, limits, capabilities should be maintained by "those who know them best and update them most often"—and that's not opencode's developers, it's the models.dev community. opencode only "consumes" this catalog, handing the grunt work of "maintaining facts" to an upstream dedicated to it. Don't reinvent the wheel, and don't re-maintain facts either—a higher-order reuse.

catalog: from "model name" to "a configured Route"

The fetched models.dev catalog is raw data; to be truly usable it must become a quickly-queryable in-memory structure—that's what catalog.ts (CatalogV2) does. It organizes the catalog into a two-level table:

model name
anthropic/claude-sonnet
split →
providerID + modelID
catalog lookup →
ModelInfo
capabilities/cost/limits/which Route
→
configured Route
lesson 33's orthogonal pieces

catalog's core is a providers: Map<ProviderID, ProviderRecord>, each ProviderRecord holding a models: Map<ModelID, ModelInfo>. So resolving "a model name" is two dictionary lookups: first find the provider by providerID, then the specific model info by modelID. Not found? catalog gives two typed errors that cleanly separate the two failure cases:

ProviderNotFoundError

even the provider isn't found (e.g. the provider segment of the model name is misspelled). The first lookup fails.

ModelNotFoundError

the provider exists but has no such model. The second lookup fails, the error carrying providerID + modelID.

Note this design's thoughtfulness: it doesn't return a vague null leaving the upper layer to guess "which step failed," but tells you clearly "is it the provider not found, or the provider exists but this model not found." The two failures' fixes differ entirely—the former you check the provider name, the latter the model name—and separating them with different error types lets the upper layer give precise hints. This design of cleanly separating failures echoes the book's recurring "let errors say who they are." This resolution step's output ModelInfo holds the key to "which Route this model should ride"—and a Route is lesson 33's composition of orthogonal pieces. Now a user-entered model name finally connects to a complete, configured pipeline from protocol to transport.

Copilot: reuse the OpenAI protocol, bring your own gate

Taking the final bow is GitHub Copilot, a special provider. Special how? Open packages/llm/src/providers/github-copilot.ts and at first glance you'll see it imports the OpenAIChat and OpenAIResponses protocol modules and reuses their ready-made .route (lesson 31's Chat and Responses)—protocol and whole Route taken wholesale! Meaning "how requests encode, how responses decode, how bytes are framed," Copilot rewrites not a word. So where is it "special"? In auth:

protocolreuse OpenAI Chat / Responses (lesson 31), zero rewrite
endpointpoints to Copilot's own baseURL
authGitHub OAuth badge → exchange for a short-lived Copilot token → Bearer header
headersattach Copilot-specific custom headers

See it? Copilot is the last and most beautiful field demonstration of lesson 33's orthogonal pieces: the protocol piece turned to "OpenAI-compatible" (reused), the endpoint piece to Copilot's address, the auth piece to the distinctive "GitHub exchanges a token" flow. Lay it next to OpenAI official and which pieces changed, which didn't, is plain:

KnobOpenAI officialGitHub Copilot
protocolopenai-chat / responsessame (entirely reused!)
endpointapi.openai.comCopilot's own baseURL
authfixed API keyGitHub badge exchanges short-lived token
transport / framingHTTP / SSEsame

It's an upgraded version of lesson 31's "openai-compatible only changes the endpoint"—this time not only the endpoint but a whole new auth, yet the protocol is still reused untouched. Copilot's auth is more complex than a plain API key: you can't just hold a fixed secret, but must first use GitHub's OAuth badge to exchange for a time-limited Copilot-specific token, then use it for Bearer auth. Precisely because auth was designed early as an independent piece, this special "exchange first, then enter" flow can be stuffed entirely into the auth piece, disturbing the protocol layer not at all. Had auth logic been entangled with protocol codec, a "same-protocol, different-auth" creature like Copilot would force you to copy the OpenAI protocol again—and orthogonal decomposition dissolves it elegantly into nothing. Using one of the trickiest real providers, it stamps a seal on all of M6's design philosophy.

Why the whole roundabout "exchange first, then enter"? Because this token exchange reflects a more secure design: your GitHub OAuth credential is long-lived and precious, and shouldn't be repeatedly sent to inference endpoints; while the exchanged Copilot token is short-lived and purpose-limited, so even if leaked, the damage is tightly bounded by expiry and scope. This is the "least privilege + short-lived credential" security convention. All opencode does is encapsulate this exchange flow into the auth piece—the upper layer just says "I want Copilot," and the underlying "badge-for-pass" round trip is quietly digested by the auth layer, the protocol layer and agent loop oblivious throughout. The complexity of security is properly isolated in the layer where it belongs, without seeping elsewhere.

🔬 Source Detail

The trace of Copilot reusing OpenAI's two Routes and only .with(...)-overriding endpoint + auth is clear in providers/github-copilot.ts:

// simplified from packages/llm/src/providers/github-copilot.ts
import * as OpenAIChat from "../protocols/openai-chat"
import * as OpenAIResponses from "../protocols/openai-responses"

// take OpenAI's ready-made routes, .with(...) overriding only endpoint + auth
const chatRoute = OpenAIChat.route.with({ provider: id })
const configuredChat = chatRoute.with({
  endpoint: { baseURL: options.baseURL },     // ← Copilot's own address
  auth: AuthOptions.bearer(options, []),       // ← the exchanged token as Bearer
})

What the whole file does is essentially "take OpenAI's ready-made routes and .with(...)-override only endpoint and auth." The chat and responses routes directly reuse OpenAIChat.route / OpenAIResponses.route—protocol and framing untouched. All the "Copilot flavor" is concentrated in the two pieces of configuration endpoint (baseURL) and auth (bearer token), not in code logic. It's an excellent example of "differences should manifest as configuration, not branches": Copilot's difference from OpenAI didn't become a pile of if (isCopilot) scattered through protocol code, but converged into clean config differences like "swap a baseURL, swap an auth." Cage the difference in configuration, and the protocol code can forever stay single-copy.

🗺️ The Big Picture · M6 Panoramic Finale

This lesson completes model resolution, and the whole M6 (LLM protocol layer) loop formally closes. Looking back over this entire part:

  • L28 anti-corruption layer: core speaks only canonical (LLMRequest out / LLMEvent back), adapters wall off with translation.
  • L29 protocol skeleton: every protocol fills the same "two-column form" (body request side + stream response-side state machine).
  • L30–32 four dialects: Anthropic (blocks + cache budget), OpenAI (Chat/Responses dual protocol + 24-line ecosystem free-ride), Gemini+Bedrock (renamed dialect + binary transport / nesting-doll state machine).
  • L33 transport & building blocks: a Route is assembled from four orthogonal pieces (protocol/endpoint/auth/framing) + id, orthogonal and composable (the HTTP/WS transport choice is an extension of the framing axis).
  • L34 events and caching: LLMEvent (inbound canonical vocabulary, completing the translation wall) + cache-policy (cache the stable prefix, connecting Context Epoch).
  • L35 resolution and Copilot: models.dev (externalized model encyclopedia, factual bedrock) → catalog (model name → configured Route); Copilot reuses OpenAI's Route, swapping only endpoint+auth, sealing this building-block architecture.

In one sentence: the user types a model name, catalog resolves it via models.dev's facts into a Route with its pieces configured; core sends a request in canonical, the Route's protocol translates it into dialect, transport puts it on the wire, framing cuts frames, the protocol decodes the response back into a canonical LLMEvent stream for the agent loop, and caching throughout is kept cheap by cache-policy and Context Epoch joining forces. Seven lessons in, you'll find not one part of the LLM layer is "for showing off"—every layer of abstraction solves a real pain point, which is exactly the engineering beauty worth savoring repeatedly. The next part (M7) turns to the tool system—how the tools the agent loop wields to actually "do work" (read/write files, run commands, search…) are defined, executed, isolated. The LLM is the agent's "mouth and brain," tools its "hands."

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • models.dev = an externalized community model catalog (models-dev.ts fetches via HTTP, caches, emits models-dev.refreshed): each Model records capabilities (attachment/reasoning/tool_call…), cost (input/output/cache-read/write prices, lesson 34's reckoning data source), limit (context/input/output caps), modalities. Blocking volatile model facts out of the code, a new model is often recognized with zero changes.
  • catalog (catalog.ts CatalogV2) = the resolver: providers: Map<ProviderID, {provider, models: Map<ModelID, ModelInfo>}>. A model name via two dictionary lookups → ModelInfo (incl. which Route); not found gives typed ProviderNotFoundError / ModelNotFoundError.
  • The full resolution chain: model name → split providerID+modelID → catalog finds ModelInfo → lesson 33's building-block Route → ready to stream.
  • Copilot = the ultimate building-block demo (packages/llm/src/providers/github-copilot.ts): directly reuses OpenAI's Route (OpenAIChat.route/OpenAIResponses.route, zero rewrite), .with(...)-overriding only the endpoint (Copilot baseURL) and auth (AuthOptions.bearer, token obtained via GitHub OAuth exchange) pieces.
  • Difference is configuration, not branches: Copilot's difference from OpenAI converges into "swap headers + baseURL" config, not if (isCopilot) in protocol code. Because auth was designed early as an independent piece, the special "exchange first, then enter" auth disturbs the protocol layer not at all. M6's loop now closes; next stop M7, the tool system.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why doesn't opencode hardcode "a model's context size, input/output price" in code, but fetch models.dev instead?
  1. Because models.dev runs faster
  2. These facts keep changing with vendor releases; externalizing to a community-maintained, self-updating catalog means a new model is often recognized with zero code changes, blocking "volatile facts" out of the code
  3. Purely to add a network dependency
  4. Because you can't put numbers in code
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Model limits, prices, capabilities change frequently with vendor releases; hardcoding means changing code and shipping each time. opencode fetches models.dev instead — a community-maintained public catalog covering nearly all LLMs, cached and emitting models-dev.refreshed on refresh. It's the "keep change out of the code" wisdom (same root as lesson 31's OpenAI-compatible free-ride), and hands the grunt work of "maintaining facts" to the upstream community that knows them best — not re-maintaining facts is a higher-order reuse.
2. When catalog fails to resolve a "model name," it gives two distinct errors, ProviderNotFoundError and ModelNotFoundError, not one null. The benefit?
  1. To make the code longer
  2. The two errors are pure redundancy
  3. More errors look more professional
  4. Clearly distinguishes "provider not found" from "provider exists but model not found"—their fixes differ, so separated, the upper layer can give precise hints
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. catalog is a two-level dictionary: find provider by providerID, then model by modelID. The two failures' fixes differ entirely — ProviderNotFound you check the provider segment, ModelNotFound you check the model segment (error carries providerID+modelID). Separating them with distinct error types, rather than a vague null leaving the upper layer to guess "which step failed," lets the upper layer give precise hints. Echoes the book's "let errors say who they are."
3. GitHub Copilot as a "special provider"—its "specialness" mainly shows in which Route piece?
  1. auth (and endpoint): protocol entirely reuses OpenAI Chat/Responses, swapping only the endpoint + a distinctive "GitHub badge exchanges short-lived token" auth
  2. framing: it uses binary framing
  3. Every Route piece differs from everyone
  4. protocol: it invented a brand-new protocol
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. packages/llm/src/providers/github-copilot.ts directly reuses OpenAIChat.route / OpenAIResponses.route — protocol and framing zero rewrite. Its "specialness" is all in auth: no fixed API key, but first exchange a GitHub OAuth badge for a time-limited Copilot token then Bearer-auth (AuthOptions.bearer), with endpoint also pointing to Copilot's own. This is lesson 33's building-block Route's best field test: reuse the protocol piece, .with(...)-swap only endpoint+auth. The difference converges into "swap baseURL+auth" config, not if(isCopilot) in the protocol.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says opencode externalizes facts like "model prices, limits, capabilities" to the models.dev community catalog to maintain, only consuming them—calling it the higher-order reuse of "not re-maintaining facts." From a project you've done, give an example of a fact that "could have been externalized to an authoritative upstream but was hardcoded and self-maintained" (e.g. timezone tables, exchange rates, country codes, a third-party API's fields). What benefits and what new risks (upstream unavailability, data-format drift) would externalizing bring?
  • Copilot's auth uses "token exchange": take the long-lived GitHub OAuth credential, exchange for a short-lived, purpose-limited Copilot token, then authenticate with it. The lesson calls this the "least privilege + short-lived credential" security convention, with the whole complex flow encapsulated into the auth piece, undisturbing the protocol layer. Argue: why is "not sending the long-lived credential to inference endpoints, but exchanging for a short-lived token" more secure? What does isolating this security complexity in the auth layer (rather than scattering it) do for the system's maintainability and auditability?

Defining a tool

M6 finished the "LLM protocol layer"—the agent's mouth and brain: how to talk to models, translating speech into each vendor's dialect. But an agent that only talks isn't enough; it must actually do work: read files, edit code, run commands, search… These "do-work" capabilities are tools, the agent's hands. This whole part, M7, covers opencode's tool system. And this first lesson asks the most foundational question: what exactly is "a tool"? How is one defined? The answer is surprisingly clean—a tool is just filling out a form called Tool.make, and that form's soul is a schema that has the final say at every gate.

Reading this lesson, you'll repeatedly recall two old friends from M6: lesson 29's "every protocol fills the same two-column form," and lesson 22's "a codec wears three hats." A tool's design is the same wisdom recurring—every tool fills the same Config form (whether read or bash), and one schema governs three things at once: telling the model how to call it, validating the input the model passes in, validating the output the tool spits out. Grasp these two points and you hold the key to the whole tool system—all the concrete tools in L38–L40 are just "different fillings of this form."

🔬 Analogy
Picture a tool as a precision lab instrument with a spec sheet. The model (user) can't directly fiddle with the instrument's wiring—it can only: first read the spec sheet (what inputs the instrument takes, what outputs it produces, i.e. the JSON schema), fill out a request form accordingly (the input), and hand it to the dispatcher (settle). The dispatcher does three things: one, check the form is filled correctly (decode-validate against the input schema; if wrong, bounce it back); two, run the instrument (execute); three, check the instrument's output meets spec (encode-validate against the output schema), then hand back two copies—a structured record (for the files) and a plain-language summary (for the model to read). As for the instrument's internal wiring, it's locked in a dedicated cabinet (a WeakMap)—the "instrument" you hold is really just a key tag, the actual mechanism you can't touch, only operate through the dispatcher's slots. This "spec sheet + request form + dispatcher + locked mechanism" is the full picture of one opencode tool.

One definition form: Tool.make's Config

Open core/src/tool/tool.ts and the entry to defining a tool is one function Tool.make(config). What does this config look like? Clean as a table—every tool fills this same form:

description
what the tool does (this text goes straight to the model—its basis for deciding whether to call it)
input
the input Schema (codec): what parameters this tool takes
output
the output Schema (codec): what result this tool produces
execute
the function that actually works: (input, context) => Effect<output, ToolFailure>
toModelOutput?
optional: render the result into content the model sees (text / file)

The form's core three are input / output / execute: declaring "what it takes, what it produces, how it computes." execute's signature (input, context) => Effect<output, ToolFailure> reeks of this book's spirit—typed input and output (inferred from the input/output schemas), errors as values not exceptions (ToolFailure, echoing lessons 5, 8), plus a context. This is the same design philosophy as lesson 29's protocol "two-column form": nail a category's commonality into one form, and each instance just fills in the blanks. The read tool, bash tool, grep tool… wildly different, all filling this same Config.

What's that tagging-along context? It's the "identity four-pack" every tool execution receives, anchoring this call back to its session and agent:

sessionID
which session this call belongs to (back to lesson 14's session data model)
agent
which agent is calling (back to M8's agents)
assistantMessageID
under which assistant message it hangs
toolCallID
which tool call this is (a turn may have many)

Don't underestimate these four fields: they let a tool "know where it is." A read tool reading "the current session's working directory," a permission check asking "does this agent allow it," an output hanging onto "the right message"—all rely on context. A tool isn't a free-floating pure function; it grows on a session tree, and context is the thread tying it to the root.

The schema wears three hats: final say at every gate

This lesson's must-take insight is the weight of the two schemas input/output. They aren't just "type annotations" but a contract guarding three gates of the tool lifecycle at once:

① shown to the model
definition(name) turns input/output schemas into JSON Schema, sent with description to the model—so it knows "how to call this tool"
② validating input in
settle first decodes the model's raw params via the input schema; invalid → ToolFailure, not a crash
③ validating output out
after execute, encode-validate via the output schema; a tool spitting the wrong shape → also ToolFailure

See it? The same schema, declared once, guards three gates: outward, it's the model's "user manual"; inward, it's the input's "security checkpoint" and the output's "quality control." This is lesson 22's "codec wears three hats" recurring at the tool layer—you only declare "this tool's input looks like this, output like that," and "how to generate the manual, how to validate, how to serialize" all come for free. Even nicer is its handling of failure: the model often passes params that don't fit the schema (hallucinations, typos), and a tool may, due to a bug, spit out a non-conforming result—both kinds of "dirty data" are stopped at the gate into a well-behaved ToolFailure value, rather than an exception blasting through the whole agent loop. The schema is the line of defense between the tool and a chaotic world.

①decodedecodeUnknown(input)(call.input): validate the model's params → failure is ToolFailure
②executeconfig.execute(input, context): actually do the work
③encodeencode(output)(result): validate the output shape → failure is ToolFailure
④two copies{ structured: the structured result, content: text/file shown to the model }

That final step ④ is worth savoring too: settle emits not one lump but two copies—structured (structured result, for the system to file) and content (content for the model to read). Where does content come from? If the tool provides toModelOutput, use it to render; otherwise, if the output is a string show it as text, if not give nothing. This is the divide-and-conquer of "structure for the system, plain language for the model"—again echoing lesson 22's "prose to communicate, structure to remember."

structured (for the system)

The structured value after output-schema encoding, filed verbatim into message history for later programmatic reading, replay, audit.

content (for the model)

The text/file list rendered by toModelOutput—holding only the side the model should see. A tool can "record a lot internally, show the model one line."

Why separate these two? Because "what the system records" and "what the model should see" are often not the same. E.g. a file-read tool's structured result might hold full byte counts, line numbers, encoding (useful for the system's files), but the model's content needs only the file content itself; or some tools' internal result is huge yet want to tell the model just "done, wrote 42 lines." Decoupling structured and content lets a tool be "detailed inward, concise outward" as needed—which also foreshadows lesson 42's "bounded output": the side shown to the model can be trimmed.

The opaque handle: a key tag + a locked mechanism

One detail so cleverly designed it's easy to miss: what exactly is the "tool" that Tool.make returns? You might assume an object with methods. But look at the source—it returns Object.freeze({}), a frozen empty object! So where's the real behavior (definition/settle/permission) hidden? In a module-level WeakMap<AnyTool, Runtime>:

Tool.make(config)
returns Object.freeze({})
register →
WeakMap<tool, Runtime>
the real definition/settle live here
Tool.settle(tool,…) looks up →
retrieve Runtime, run
outsiders can't touch the mechanism

Why so roundabout? Because this way, the "tool" value is, to the outside, a pure "key tag"—its type Definition<Input, Output> carries only phantom type info (a [TypeId] remembering what Input/Output are), with zero runtime surface for outsiders to fiddle with. Holding a tool, you can do nothing but hand it to Tool.settle / Tool.definition. This is information hiding at its elegant extreme: the value is a capability token, the behavior locked in a private registry. The decorator withPermission(tool, "permName") follows naturally—it makes a new key tag pointing at a "permissioned" Runtime in the WeakMap (lesson 41's permission system plugs in right here). And using a WeakMap has a hidden perk: when a tool is GC'd, its Runtime is released automatically, no memory leak.

This "value is a token, behavior lives elsewhere" style looks roundabout but is deeply sensible on reflection. It buys three things: one, immutability—whoever holds a tool can't change its behavior (an empty object has no method to override), only use it by the rules; two, decoration yields a new value—withPermission doesn't mutate the original tool but derives a new key tag pointing at the enhanced Runtime, the original untouched (immutable thinking, lesson 24's refrain); three, types and runtime fully separated—the type layer has only the two phantom params Input/Output, by which the compiler guarantees the input you pass and output you receive line up, while the runtime grunt work hides in the WeakMap without polluting the types. A seemingly odd "return an empty object" actually buys "immutable, derivable, type-clean" all in one.

A passing note on the tool-name bar: validateName requires the name match /^[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_-]{0,63}$/—letter-led, up to 64 chars, only alphanumerics and -_. Why care? Because the tool name goes verbatim into the JSON Schema sent to the model, and also serves as a key in permissions and the registry; a clean, regular naming constraint blocks a heap of weird-character trouble at the source.

🗺️ The Big Picture

This lesson lays the foundation of the whole tool system:

  • A tool = filling a Config form (Tool.make): description (read by the model) + input/output (schema) + execute (do work) + optional toModelOutput. Every tool fills this same form, just as lesson 29's every protocol fills the "two-column form."
  • The schema wears three hats: ① definition turns it into JSON Schema as the model's manual; ② settle decode-validates incoming params via the input schema; ③ encode-validates the outgoing result via the output schema. Dirty input/output is stopped into a ToolFailure value, not blasting the agent loop.
  • The settle pipeline: decode input → execute → encode output → emit {structured, content} two copies (structure for the system, plain language for the model).
  • Opaque handle + WeakMap: make returns a frozen empty object, the real Runtime locked in WeakMap<AnyTool,Runtime>; a tool is a "capability token," only usable via settle/definition. withPermission derives a new handle layering permission.

With this "shape of a tool," the rest flows: lesson 37 covers the tool registry (how these defined tools are collected, materialized per Location into a model-visible list); lessons 38–40 cover concrete tools (read/write/edit, glob/grep/bash, webfetch/question/todowrite—all different fillings of this Config form); lesson 41 covers permissions (the line withPermission planted); lesson 42 covers bounded output (how execute's large results get trimmed). This lesson's Tool.make is the origin point of all that follows.

🔬 Source Detail

settle's Effect chain strings "validate—execute—revalidate" beautifully (simplified from tool.ts):

// settle: a decode → execute → encode pipeline
Schema.decodeUnknownEffect(config.input)(call.input).pipe(
  Effect.mapError(e => new ToolFailure({ message: `Invalid tool input: ...` })),
  Effect.flatMap(input =>
    config.execute(input, context).pipe(        // actually do the work
      Effect.flatMap(output =>
        Schema.encodeEffect(config.output)(output)   // validate output shape
          .pipe(Effect.mapError(e => new ToolFailure({ ... }))),
      ),
      Effect.map(output => ({ structured: output, content: ... })),
    ),
  ),
)

Note the two mapErrors converge both failure kinds into the same ToolFailure: invalid input, non-conforming output—to the upper layer, a tool failure is one uniform value, and the agent loop (lesson 17) receiving it can handle gracefully (pass the error back to the model to retry), without distinguishing "did the model pass wrong or the tool compute wrong." One more closing nicety: when a tool gives no toModelOutput and its output is itself a string, content defaults to handing that string straight as text to the model—zero burden for the simple "output is text" tool. Complexity for those who need it, simplicity returned to the majority—this thoughtfulness in defaults is the mark of a good API.

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • Tools are the agent's "hands": M6 is mouth and brain (talking to models), M7 is hands (read/write files, run commands, search). This lesson asks "how is a tool defined."
  • Tool.make(config) is one form (core/src/tool/tool.ts): description (read by model) / input / output (schema) / execute: (input, context) => Effect<output, ToolFailure> / optional toModelOutput. Every tool fills this same form (like lesson 29's protocol two-column form).
  • The schema wears three hats: ① definition(name) → JSON Schema as the model's manual; ② settle decode-validates input via the input schema; ③ encode-validates output via the output schema. Dirty input/output converge into a ToolFailure value (no blasting the loop). A recurrence of lesson 22's "codec wears three hats."
  • settle pipeline: decode → execute → encode → {structured (system files), content (model-readable text/file)}. With no toModelOutput and a string output, content defaults to that string.
  • Opaque handle + WeakMap: make returns Object.freeze({}), the real Runtime (definition/settle/permission) hidden in module-level WeakMap<AnyTool, Runtime>. A tool is a "capability token," only usable via Tool.settle/definition; withPermission derives a new handle layering permission (lesson 41 plug-in). Context = {sessionID, agent, assistantMessageID, toolCallID}.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What is "defining a tool" in opencode based on?
  1. Adding a case to one giant switch
  2. Filling a Tool.make Config form: description (read by the model) + input/output (schema) + execute (do work) + optional toModelOutput — every tool fills this same form
  3. Subclassing a BaseTool abstract class and overriding many methods
  4. Writing a standalone microservice
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Tool.make(config) (core/src/tool/tool.ts) is the sole entry to defining a tool. Config's core three are input/output/execute: declaring "what it takes, produces, how it computes," with execute's signature (input, context) => Effect typed I/O and errors-as-values. Same design philosophy as lesson 29's "every protocol fills the two-column form" — nail the commonality into one form, each instance just fills blanks. read/bash/grep, wildly different, all fill this same Config.
2. What does a tool's input/output schema do all at once?
  1. Only for documentation
  2. Three: ① definition turns it into JSON Schema as the model's manual; ② settle decode-validates incoming params via the input schema; ③ encode-validates the outgoing result via the output schema
  3. Only validates input, ignores output
  4. Only for generating TypeScript types, not involved at runtime
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The same schema, declared once, guards three gates: outward the model's manual (definition→JSON Schema), inward the input's checkpoint (decode) and output's QC (encode). A recurrence of lesson 22's "codec wears three hats" at the tool layer. The key payoff is failure handling: the model passing schema-violating params, a tool spitting non-conforming output — both dirty-data kinds are stopped into a well-behaved ToolFailure value, not an exception blasting the agent loop. The schema is the tool's line of defense against a chaotic world.
3. Tool.make's returned "tool" is actually Object.freeze({}) (a frozen empty object), the real behavior hidden in a module-level WeakMap. Why this design?
  1. To save memory
  2. Purely a bug
  3. Because TypeScript doesn't support classes
  4. Make the tool an immutable "capability token": only usable via Tool.settle/definition; withPermission derives a new handle instead of mutating; the type layer has only phantom Input/Output params, runtime grunt work not polluting types
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Returning an empty object with behavior in a WeakMap buys three goods at once: ① immutability — holders can't change a tool's behavior, only use it by the rules; ② decoration yields a new value — withPermission derives a new key tag pointing at the enhanced Runtime, the original untouched (immutable thinking); ③ types/runtime separated — the type layer keeps only the two phantom Input/Output params for the compiler to align, runtime hidden in the WeakMap without polluting types. WeakMap also throws in: a GC'd tool's Runtime auto-releases, no leak. withPermission is exactly lesson 41's permission plug-in point.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says settle emits two copies—structured (the structured value filed for the system) and content (text/file read by the model)—deliberately decoupled: "what the system records" and "what the model should see" are often not the same (e.g. file-read: structured has byte counts/line numbers/encoding, the model needs only the content). From an interface you've designed, discuss why this dual output of "detailed inward, concise outward for the same operation's result" is useful. How would it cooperate with lesson 42's "bounded tool output"?
  • The lesson likens a tool's execute signature (input, context) => Effect to lesson 29's protocol "two-column form," both "nailing a category's commonality into one form so each instance just fills blanks." Give two more examples you know of unifying a family of implementations via "the same form/interface, each filling blanks" (e.g. plugin systems, web middleware, database drivers). Versus "each implementation doing its own thing," what does "defining one common shape" buy for adding, testing, and reuse respectively?

The tool registry

Last lesson we learned to define a tool (that Tool.make form). But how do defined tools get collected, turned into a list the model can actually see and call? That's the tool registry (ToolRegistry)'s job. It's a bridge: one end to "a pile of scattered defined tools" (L36), the other to "which tools this conversation turn should show the model, and who an order gets dispatched to" (what lesson 17's agent loop uses). The registry splits this bridge into two actions: register—tools check in; materialize—print, on the spot per current conditions, a "today's menu" for the model.

Reading this lesson, you'll strongly recall lesson 23's "System Context Registry"—they're almost the same wisdom appearing twice: both use Scope binding to manage "who's in, who's out," the scope closing auto-revokes the registration, never leaving ghosts. But the tool registry adds two more refined designs: "same-named tools stack up, the latest on top" (supporting override and auto-restore), and "at materialize time, filter by permission and stamp an anti-forgery seal" (banned tools don't even enter the menu; preventing "ordering a stale dish"). Grasp these two and you understand how opencode, in a dynamic world where "plugins come and go anytime, permissions change anytime," still hands the model a clean, current, trustworthy tool list.

🍽️ Analogy
Picture the registry as a restaurant's front-desk roster + today's-menu machine. A cook (tool) coming on duty must clock in (register)—but clocking in is "shift-bound": while your shift is on, your name's on the roster; the shift ends and it vanishes automatically, no one needing to remember to cross it off. If two cooks share a name (a "signature edition" covering the "basic edition"), the roster stacks them, and the latest clock-in always works the shift; when they clock out, the one beneath auto-resurfaces. Whenever a table of guests (the model) is seated, the front desk prints "today's menu" on the spot (materialize): it scans the current roster, crosses off today's banned dishes (permission filter), and prints two things—a menu for the guests (definitions) and a dispatch desk (settle, an order routed to the right cook). And every dish on the menu is stamped with today's anti-forgery seal: should a guest order off an old menu while that cook has long clocked out and been replaced, the dispatch desk sees the seal mismatch and stops it cold—"this is a stale dish."

register: clock in, bound to scope

First register(tools)—register a batch of tools (a {name: tool} dict). Its implementation is full of lesson 23's shadow, but with more finesse:

register(tools)
①validate →
validateName
each name conforms
②stack →
local[name].push({token, tool})
same name stacks up
③cleanup →
addFinalizer
scope closes→revoke by token

Three plain-looking steps, all detail. One, each name first passes validateName (lesson 36's /^[A-Za-z]...{0,63}$/ rule)—non-conforming names don't get in, keeping the menu clean at the source. Two, local isn't Map<name, tool> but Map<name, tool array>—under one name is a stack of registrations. Why a stack? Because it supports override: a plugin wanting to cover the built-in read with its own just registers another same-named tool pressed on top; retrieval always takes .at(-1) (top of stack, the latest). Three, and most crucial—Effect.addFinalizer binds the scope: registration generates a unique token = {}, and when the scope closes, the finalizer precisely pulls its own registration out of the stack by that token. After pulling, if the stack is empty delete the name, if there's still (the one it covered) it auto-resurfaces to work the shift.

This "stack + revoke-by-token" combo yields an elegant property: a tool's "existence" lives and dies with its scope, and overrides auto-restore. A plugin registers a read override in its scope; the moment the plugin unloads (scope closes), the override vanishes automatically and the built-in auto-restores—no manual "remember to unregister" burden, and no possibility of "plugin's gone but its tool lingers" ghosts. This is exactly the extension of lesson 23's acquireRelease thinking: weld "cleanup" onto "acquisition," letting the lifecycle manage itself.

materialize: print a "today's menu" on the spot

When a conversation turn is about to begin, the agent loop needs "which tools the model can use this turn." That's what materialize(permissions) does—it doesn't return the ever-changing local, but takes a snapshot right now, processed into a currently-usable list:

applications
app-level resident tools
overlay local tops →
all current tools
each name's latest
filter by permission →
remove whollyDisabled
banned not in menu
produce →
{definitions, settle}

Three steps: first merge app-level resident tools (applications) with each name's stack top in local, getting "all tools right now"; then run the passed permission ruleset over them, removing whollyDisabled (fully-disabled) tools from the list; finally produce two things:

definitions
each surviving tool's ToolDefinition (JSON Schema + description)—this is the "menu" sent to the model
settle
a dispatch function: the model orders a tool, find it by name, dispatch to execute

Here's an easy-to-skip but important design point: permission filtering happens at the "menu" layer. A tool fully banned by policy doesn't even appear in the definitions sent to the model—the model doesn't even know "such a tool exists," so naturally won't order it. This is far cleaner than "let the model order then refuse": the best refusal is to not let it know the option exists (lesson 41's permission system goes deep on this). On the other side, materialize produces a snapshot—it freezes "which tools exist right now, and each one's identity," decoupled from the still-scope-changing local. register owns "which tools exist" (mutable, scoped), materialize owns "which this turn sees" (immutable snapshot), cleanly separated:

register · registry (mutable)

The "live" state constantly added/removed with plugins and Location scopes; same-name stacking, latest on shift; it asks "which tools exist in the world right now."

materialize · materialization (immutable)

A snapshot taken at one moment + permission filter + identity freeze; it asks "this turn, which the model should see and can call." Once printed, unchanging this turn.

Dispatch & anti-forgery: settle's "stale dish" interception

The menu's printed, the model orders a dish (initiates a tool call), and settle handles dispatch + execution + cleanup. This chain collects all the prior lessons' planted seeds:

①findby call.name find the stack-top registration (or in applications)
②verify sealdoes the identity recorded at materialize match the current? mismatch→"Stale tool call"
③executesettle(tool, call, context): lesson 36's decode→execute→encode
④catch errorcatch ToolFailure → becomes {type:error, value:message} back to the model
⑤boundresources.bound(...): large output truncated/spilled (lesson 42)

Step ② "verify seal" shows the most craft. Between "the model saw the menu" and "the model orders this dish," there's a time gap—during which that tool's registration may well have changed (its scope closed, or it was re-registered into something else). How to guarantee what the model orders is exactly the one it saw on the menu? Via a unique identity = {} object each registration carries: materialize records it into the menu, and settle before executing checks "is the identity under this name still the one on the menu." Mismatch → decisively report "Stale tool call," never misattributing the call to a same-named tool whose identity has changed. This is a subtle but important correctness guarantee—in a system where tools come and go dynamically, it preserves "what you see is what you call." Imagine without this seal check: the model reads the menu's read (built-in), about to call it, and right then a plugin registers its own read pressed on top; without identity verification, this call would silently get dispatched to the plugin's tool the model never saw—behavior mismatching the model's expectation, with no awareness. The seal turns this "same name, different person" mismatch into an explicit, observable error, not a silent uncanny event.

Steps ④⑤ are the interfaces to later lessons: ④ collects the tool's ToolFailure into an "error result value" back to the model (let it see the error and retry), rather than an exception blasting the loop—the upper layer (lesson 17's agent loop) always receives a well-behaved result. ⑤ hands the execution result to ToolOutputStore for "bounding": a grep might spit tens of thousands of lines, and stuffing it straight back into the conversation would blow the context, so here it's truncated, with the full text spilled to a managed file (lesson 42's star). The registry is the point where these two cross-cutting concerns (permissions, bounded output) mount onto the tool-execution flow.

🗺️ The Big Picture

The tool registry is the bridge between "defined tools" and "tools the agent loop uses":

  • register (mutable·scoped): validateName → same-name stacks up (latest on shift) → addFinalizer revokes by token. Scope closes→registration auto-vanishes, the covered one auto-restores. An extension of lesson 23's acquireRelease thinking.
  • materialize (immutable·snapshot): applications + local stack-tops → remove whollyDisabled by permission → produce {definitions (menu for the model), settle (dispatch desk)}. Permission filtering at the menu layer: a banned tool is invisible to the model.
  • settle (dispatch·anti-forgery·cleanup): find by name → verify identity against "stale dish" (Stale tool call) → lesson 36's decode/execute/encode → catch ToolFailure into an error value → ToolOutputStore.bound truncates large output.
  • builtins.locationLayer: uses Layer.mergeAll to compose the built-in tools' (read/write/edit/bash/glob/grep/question/todowrite/webfetch/websearch/skill/apply-patch) layers, each self-registering Location-scoped.

The register/materialize line cleanly cuts "which tools exist" from "which this turn sees," giving permissions (lesson 41) and bounded output (lesson 42) clear mount points. So where do these built-in tools "check in"? The answer is locationLayer in builtins.ts—it uses Layer.mergeAll to merge all shipped built-in tools' layers into one, each tool's layer self-registering into the registry as it's assembled:

file
read / write / edit / apply-patch (lesson 38)
search & exec
glob / grep / bash (lesson 39)
other
webfetch / websearch / question / todowrite (lesson 40)
Skills
skill (lesson 43, a permissioned special tool)

Note the Location in locationLayer's name—these tools are registered per Location (workspace/filesystem scope). This lines up with "register binds to scope": each Location has its own set of tool registrations, and when the Location's lifecycle ends, this tool set exits with it. "Tools come and go with the Location" is register's scope mechanism cashed out directly on the built-in tools. From the next lesson (38–40) we dive into individual concrete tools—all different fillings of lesson 36's Config form, and their being seen and called by the model relies entirely on this lesson's registry.

🔬 Source Detail

register's "stack + revoke-by-token" is the core of this elegance (simplified from registry.ts):

// register: same-name push; on scope close, precisely revoke by token
const token = {}
for (const [name, tool] of entries)
  local.set(name, [...(local.get(name) ?? []), { token, registration: { identity: {}, tool } }])

Effect.addFinalizer(() => {
  for (const [name] of entries) {
    const rest = local.get(name)?.filter(r => r.token !== token) ?? []
    rest.length > 0 ? local.set(name, rest) : local.delete(name)  // empty→delete, rest auto-surfaces
  }
})

Note two different empty objects: token identifies "which register call" (to precisely locate this call's entries on revoke), identity identifies "which specific registration" (to verify the anti-forgery seal at settle). Using an empty object {} as a unique identifier is a seasoned little trick—an object's reference is inherently unique, {} !== {}, no UUID generation needed, no collisions, and comparison is fast (one reference-equality check). The whole block is wrapped in Effect.uninterruptible: "pushing the stack" and "installing the finalizer" must complete atomically together, never pushing the stack but, due to an interrupt, failing to install the revoke hook—else you'd leak a ghost registration that can never be revoked. Even something as small as "registration" is taken seriously by Effect's interruption semantics.

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • The registry bridges "defined tools" and "tools the agent loop uses" (core/src/tool/registry.ts), two actions: register + materialize.
  • register: validateName validates → local: Map<name, registration array> same-name stacks, .at(-1) latest on shift → Effect.addFinalizer revokes by unique token. Scope closes→registration auto-vanishes, the covered one auto-restores (extension of lesson 23's acquireRelease). Push+finalizer wrapped in uninterruptible for atomicity.
  • materialize(permissions): applications + local stack-tops merged → remove whollyDisabled tools → produce {definitions, settle}. Permission filtering at the menu layer—banned tools don't enter definitions, invisible to the model (better than "order then refuse"). It's an immutable snapshot, decoupled from the mutable local.
  • settle dispatch: find registration by call.name → verify identity against "Stale tool call" (guaranteeing "what you see is what you call") → lesson 36's decode/execute/encode → catch ToolFailure into an error value back → resources.bound truncates large output (lesson 42).
  • builtins.locationLayer: Layer.mergeAll composes built-in tools' layers, each self-registering Location-scoped. token=identifies this register call; identity=identifies a single registration (seal check); empty object {} as a natural unique identifier.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. How does the tool registry's register manage a tool's "existence"?
  1. A global Map, new registration overwrites old, never restored
  2. Scope binding + same-name stacking: registration installs addFinalizer, scope close→auto-revoke by token; same-name takes .at(-1) latest on shift, the covered one auto-restores on revoke
  3. Once registered a tool exists forever
  4. Writes tools into a database, manually added/removed
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. local is Map — same-name stacks up. register generates a unique token, pushes the registration, and addFinalizer: scope close precisely pulls this call's registration by token, deletes the name if empty, the rest auto-surfaces. So "a tool's existence lives and dies with its scope, overrides auto-restore" — the moment a plugin unloads (scope closes), its override vanishes and the built-in restores, no manual unregister. An extension of lesson 23's acquireRelease "weld cleanup onto acquisition." Push+finalizer also wrapped in uninterruptible for atomicity.
2. What happens to a tool fully disabled by permission policy after materialize?
  1. Causes materialize to error
  2. It doesn't even enter definitions — the model doesn't even know "this tool exists," so won't order it (the best refusal is to not let it know the option exists)
  3. Still enters the menu, the model orders then gets refused
  4. Enters the menu but marked "disabled"
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. materialize merges applications + local stack-tops, runs the passed permission ruleset over them, removes whollyDisabled tools, then produces {definitions, settle}. So permission filtering happens at the "menu" layer — a banned tool doesn't even appear in the definitions sent to the model, far cleaner than "order then refuse." materialize is an immutable snapshot, decoupled from the scope-changing local: register owns "which tools exist," materialize owns "which this turn sees."
3. settle before executing verifies each registration's identity (a unique empty object), reporting "Stale tool call" on mismatch. What does this prevent?
  1. Prevents two tools sharing a name
  2. Prevents SQL injection
  3. Prevents "same name, identity changed" mismatch: between the model seeing the menu and ordering, the tool's registration may have changed; the seal guarantees "what you see is what you call," not silently dispatching to a same-named tool the model never saw
  4. Prevents the model calling too often
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. In a system where tools come and go dynamically, there's a time gap between "the model saw the menu" and "the model orders this dish" — during which the tool's registration may have changed via scope close or re-registration. Each registration carries a unique identity={}, materialize records it into the menu, and settle before executing checks "is the identity under this name still the menu's one," mismatch → Stale tool call. It turns the "same name, different person" mismatch into an explicit, observable error, preserving "what you see is what you call," not a silent uncanny event.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says register (mutable·scoped "which tools exist") and materialize (immutable·snapshot "which this turn sees") are deliberately split into two layers. Argue: separating "the ever-changing registry" from "a snapshot frozen at one moment" — what does it buy for correctness (concurrency, replay) and reasoning? If the agent loop read the ever-changing local directly instead of materializing a snapshot first, what could go wrong?
  • The lesson twice uses "an empty object {} as a unique identifier": token identifies "which register call," identity identifies "which specific registration," leveraging {} !== {} (reference inherently unique). Discuss this trick's pros/cons versus "generating a UUID string" (uniqueness, performance, serializability, cross-process). Where does it fit, and where does it break down (e.g. when the identifier must be written to a log or database)?

File tools

The prior two lessons built the tool system's skeleton: how a tool is defined (lesson 36's Tool.make), how it's collected into a model-visible list (lesson 37's registry). From this lesson, we dive into individual real tools, watching "the skeleton grow flesh." First the most everyday—and most skill-testing—family: file tools: read, write (whole-write), edit (precise edit), apply_patch (batch patching). For a code-writing agent, the vast majority of actions land as these four "hands" reading and changing files.

This lesson has two layers of payoff. One, confirming lesson 36: you'll see firsthand these four tools are all different fillings of the same Config form—each declaring input/output schema + execute, permissioned (write/edit/apply_patch via withPermission "edit", read via internal permission.assert), nothing more. Two, the centerpiece: we'll unfold the edit tool's execution step by step (the spec-named worked-example), seeing how much production-grade rigor hides behind a small "change this text to that" operation—exact match, ambiguity refusal, line-ending normalization, optimistic-concurrency write. You'll come away understanding: letting AI safely change your code, the hard part was never "string replacement," but the ring of guardrails around it—"don't guess, don't err, don't clobber others."

✍️ Analogy
Picture these four file tools as a wordsmith's four implements. read is a magnifying glass—look without changing, paging through a long document (offset/limit), even "looking" at a whole drawer's worth of directory. write is a fresh sheet of paper—rewrite the whole page, the old all covered. edit is a precision correction pen—"change this sentence to that," but with a fussy temperament: the original sentence to change must match letter-for-letter (spaces and indentation count); if that sentence appears twice in the text, it stops and asks back "which one do you mean?," never guessing for you; and before writing it glances—if the page got scribbled on by someone else since you last looked, it refuses to write, lest it erase their work. apply_patch is a stack of batch work orders—"add this file, change that, delete the other," executed in one pass per the orders. Four implements, for the four granularities and shapes of change, the model picking as needed.

Four "hands": one table for the division of labor

First lay the four tools side by side, their "shape" plain—note they all fill lesson 36's Config form (input/output schema + execute + permission):

ToolinputChange granularityPermission
readpath, offset?, limit?read-only (paging, directory listing)assert read permission
writepath, contentwhole-file overwrite/createwithPermission "edit"
editpath, oldString, newString, replaceAll?exact find-replace (one or all)withPermission "edit"
apply_patchpatchTextmulti-file batch add/update/deletewithPermission "edit"

This table is itself a design lesson. The four cover a coarse-to-fine, single-to-many change spectrum: write coarsest (whole page swap), edit finest (one sentence change), apply_patch broadest (cross-file in one go), and read the only side-effect-free observer. Why so many? Because different change shapes fit different tools to save the most tokens and least err: change one line with edit (send just that line), rewrite a whole file with write (no diff needed), refactor across files with apply_patch (formed in one pass). The model, per the task, picks the handiest one itself. This is the fruit of lesson 36's "one form, each fills blanks"—four fillings, covering four real needs.

Another commonality is the path boundary: all file tools' paths resolve relative to the "active Location" (workspace); absolute paths inside the Location are accepted, but absolute paths pointing outside require extra external_directory approval. This is a security fence—the agent by default can only mess within its workspace; reaching outside needs explicit permission first.

The two endpoint tools each have cleverness worth a look. First the read-only read—far more than "read a file." Its output is a three-way union, plus offset/limit paging:

file Content
read a file; images are recognized as base64 + image mime, returned as "file" content to the multimodal model
TextPage
large text read by offset (start line) + limit (line count) paging, not stuffing the context at once
ListPage
when path points to a directory, read becomes "list directory," also pageable

One tool serving "read file / list directory," with built-in paging—abstracting "observing the filesystem" into one unified entry; the model needn't remember two tools for "read a file" and "view a directory," one read with different paths suffices. Now the other endpoint, apply_patch: its output is an applied array, each item recording one operation's type and target:

add
add a file
update
modify an existing file
delete
delete a file

One patchText can describe adds, updates, deletes across multiple files at once; apply_patch executes sequentially, logging each into applied. When the model wants "a refactor across several files," versus firing a dozen edits, one apply_patch both saves tokens and makes this group of changes a semantic whole (sequential, each logged into applied for easy "which step went wrong" inspection; note automatic rollback on failure is not yet supported).

Worked-example: a single edit's full life

Now unfold edit's execute step by step. On the surface it does one thing—"replace oldString with newString in the file." But the real execution is a pipeline lined with guardrails:

①validate firstold==new? → reject "no changes"; old==""? → reject "use write for empty" (block before touching disk)
②ask permissionpermission.assert(...): is this file allowed to change? (lesson 41)
③read sourcereadFile + decodeUtf8 (handle BOM)
④normalize line endingdetect file's \n or \r\n, convert old/new to the same
⑤count matchescountOccurrences: 0→reject "not found"; >1 & not replaceAll→reject "ambiguous"
⑥replacereplaceAll ? replace all : replace first
⑦optimistic writewriteIfUnchanged(expected=just-read bytes): reject if someone changed it

In this chain, only step ⑥, one line, actually "replaces"; all the rest are guardrails. Each guardrail corresponds to a real pit "letting AI change code" steps into:

exact match, no fuzzy
oldString must match letter-for-letter (spaces, indentation); not found → reject, never "close enough so change it"
ambiguity rejected, no guessing
same segment appearing multiple times without replaceAll → stop and ask "give more context or set replaceAll"
line endings auto-normalized
model sends \n, file may be \r\n; auto-convert to the file's, so matching doesn't falsely fail
optimistic concurrency, no clobbering
writeIfUnchanged carries "the bytes I read"; if the file changed at write time → reject, never erasing others' changes

Step ⑤'s "ambiguity refusal" shows the most character. The model says "change return x to return y," but the file has three return x—which one? A reckless tool would change the first (or all), then breed a bug; edit chooses to stop and ask back. It would rather have the model "give more context" and retry than gamble. This is profound restraint: facing ambiguity, the most responsible action isn't "guess one" but "throw the ambiguity back faithfully." Step ①'s "validate before touching disk" is the same logic—when params are obviously meaningless (old==new, old==""), reject before any side effect; the earlier the error, the cheaper. Both guardrails rest on one belief: a tool called frequently by AI and directly changing your disk must block "uncertainty" before "acting." The model hallucinates, mistypes, confidently gives a mismatched oldString—edit doesn't assume the model is always right, but explicitly intercepts each "could go wrong" into a clear rejection. This stance of "distrust input, guard everywhere" is exactly what separates a toy demo from a tool you'd dare use on a real codebase.

writeIfUnchanged: optimistic concurrency that doesn't clobber others

Step ⑦'s writeIfUnchanged deserves its own savor—the most "seasoned" stroke in this design. Imagine a time gap: edit read the file's bytes at ③, and by ⑦ wants to write back, with reading, counting, computing the replacement in between—during which another process (another agent, the user editing in their editor, a watch script) may well have changed the same file. If edit blindly writes its computed new content back, it would silently erase others' changes in that interval (the classic "lost update").

③read source bytes
expected = this
…compute replace…→
⑦writeIfUnchanged
write with expected
disk still expected?
yes→write / no→reject
compare-and-swap

writeIfUnchanged({target, expected: just-read bytes, content: new})'s semantics are "compare-and-swap": write only if "the disk content still equals what I originally read"; if changed, refuse to write, error out. This locks the concurrency risk between "read—modify—write" into one atomic operation. It takes no pessimistic lock (doesn't block others) but optimistically assumes no one touched it, verifying at write time—exactly the optimistic concurrency control (OCC) common in databases and distributed systems. A small "change a file" tool wielding database-grade concurrency rigor—that's opencode's "take every small thing seriously" character. Worth noting, this guardrail is especially crucial in a multi-agent parallel future: when several agents may touch the same codebase at once, "is what I think I'm changing still the one I read" stops being paranoia and becomes a question every write must answer. edit welds that answer into every replacement ahead of time with writeIfUnchanged—it's written not for today's single agent, but as headroom for tomorrow's concurrent world.

🗺️ The Big Picture

File tools are the most direct hands for the agent "changing the world":

  • Four hands covering the change spectrum: read (read-only, paging, directory listing) / write (whole-file overwrite) / edit (exact find-replace) / apply_patch (multi-file batch add/update/delete). Coarse-to-fine, single-to-many; the model picks the handiest by change shape.
  • All different fillings of lesson 36's Config form: each declaring input/output schema + execute, wrapped in withPermission/permission.assert. Confirming "one form, each fills blanks."
  • edit's four guardrails: exact match (no fuzzy), ambiguity refusal (multiple matches, no guessing), line-ending normalization (\n↔\r\n), optimistic-concurrency write (writeIfUnchanged doesn't clobber). Only one line actually "replaces," the rest all guardrails.
  • Path boundary: resolved relative to "active Location"; external absolute paths need external_directory approval. A security fence keeping the agent within its workspace by default.

This lesson's main theme—"letting AI safely change code, the difficulty is in guardrails not replacement"—runs through all of M7. The next lesson (39) turns to search & exec tools: glob (find files by name), grep (search by content), bash (run commands)—where bash runs via spawned subprocess (batch), another ring of fine guardrails (note: bash does NOT use PTY, the next lesson clarifies). Reading files, searching files, running commands are a coding agent's three basic skills; these two lessons cover the first two thoroughly.

🔬 Source Detail

edit's "count matches → decide by count" writes restraint plainly (simplified from edit.ts):

// ④ after normalizing line endings, ⑤ count matches, branch by count
const oldString = convertToLineEnding(input.oldString, ending)  // to the file's \n / \r\n
const replacements = countOccurrences(source.text, oldString)
if (replacements === 0)
  return new ToolFailure({ message: "Could not find oldString ... must match exactly" })
if (replacements > 1 && input.replaceAll !== true)
  return new ToolFailure({ message: "Found multiple exact matches ... set replaceAll to true" })
const replaced = input.replaceAll === true
  ? source.text.replaceAll(oldString, newString)
  : source.text.replace(oldString, newString)
// ⑦ optimistic write: land only if the file is unchanged
files.writeIfUnchanged({ target, expected: source.content, content: ... })

Note all these "rejections" go through ToolFailure—back to lessons 36, 37's loop: a tool's failure isn't an exception but a well-behaved value, collected by the registry into an "error result" back to the model. So when the model reads "could not find oldString" or "multiple matches, add context," it can correct and retry itself—the error message becomes the model's "next-step hint." A good tool is useful not only on success but, on failure, makes "why it failed, what to do" clear, because its "user" is a model that adjusts per the error message. That's why each of edit's rejection messages reads like an instruction ("must match exactly," "set replaceAll to true")—they're operating guides written for the model to read.

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • Four file tools (core/src/tool/{read,write,edit,apply-patch}.ts) cover the change spectrum: read (read-only/paging/directory listing), write (whole-file overwrite), edit (exact find-replace), apply_patch (multi-file batch add/update/delete). All different fillings of lesson 36's Config form + withPermission.
  • edit's execution is a guardrail pipeline: ①validate (old==new/empty→reject) ②permission.assert ③read+decodeUtf8(BOM) ④normalize line endings (\n↔\r\n) ⑤count matches ⑥replace ⑦optimistic write. Only step ⑥, one line, actually replaces.
