  • edit's four guardrails: exact match (incl. spaces/indentation, not found → reject); ambiguity refusal (multiple matches & not replaceAll → stop and ask, no guessing for the model); auto line-ending normalization (avoiding false mismatches); optimistic-concurrency write.
  • writeIfUnchanged(expected=read bytes) = compare-and-swap: write only if disk content still equals what was originally read, else reject—locking "read-modify-write" concurrency risk into an atomic operation, preventing lost updates (optimistic concurrency control OCC, no pessimistic lock).
  • Paths resolve relative to "active Location", external absolute paths need external_directory approval (security fence). All "rejections" go through ToolFailure back to the model; the error message is the model's operating guide.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What do opencode's four file tools read/write/edit/apply_patch cover, and how do they relate to lesson 36?
  1. A "coarse-to-fine, single-to-many" change spectrum (whole-write/precise-edit/batch-patch/read-only), all different fillings of lesson 36's Config form (input/output schema + execute + permission)
  2. Four unrelated standalone programs each implemented from scratch
  3. Only read is a tool, the rest are internal functions
  4. Actually four aliases of the same tool
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. write coarsest (whole-page swap), edit finest (one-sentence change), apply_patch broadest (cross-file in one go), read the only side-effect-free observer. Different change shapes fit different tools to save tokens and least err: change one line with edit, rewrite a whole file with write, refactor across files with apply_patch, the model picking. All four fill lesson 36's Config form — declaring input/output schema + execute, permissioned (write/edit/apply_patch via withPermission "edit", read via internal permission.assert). The fruit of "one form, each fills blanks."
2. If edit finds oldString appears multiple times in the file and the call didn't set replaceAll, what does it do?
  1. Changes a random one
  2. Changes all by default
  3. Stops and asks back: returns ToolFailure "multiple matches, provide more context or set replaceAll" — never guessing for the model which to change
  4. Changes the first by default
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Facing ambiguity, the most responsible action isn't "guess one" but "throw the ambiguity back faithfully." The model says "change return x to return y," but the file has three return x—which? A reckless tool changing the first or all breeds a bug; edit stops and returns ToolFailure, having the model give more context and retry. This rejection (like "could not find oldString, must match exactly") goes through ToolFailure, the error message itself the model's operating guide. This separates a toy demo from a tool you'd dare use on a real codebase.
3. edit finally lands via writeIfUnchanged({target, expected: just-read bytes, content}). What for?
  1. Compare-and-swap: write only if disk content still equals what was originally read, else reject — locking "read-modify-write" concurrency risk into an atomic operation, preventing lost updates (optimistic concurrency, no pessimistic lock)
  2. A purely redundant check
  3. To write faster
  4. To compress the file
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. From ③ reading bytes to ⑦ writing back, counting and computing the replacement intervene—during which another process (another agent, the user editing, a watch script) may have changed the same file. Blindly writing would silently erase their changes (lost update). writeIfUnchanged carries expected=the read bytes, semantics compare-and-swap: write only if disk still equals expected, reject if changed. It takes no pessimistic lock (doesn't block others), optimistically assumes no one touched it, verifying at write—databases'/distributed systems' OCC. Especially crucial in a multi-agent parallel future.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says in edit's execution "only one line actually replaces, the rest all guardrails," mapping four guardrails (exact match/ambiguity refusal/line-ending normalization/optimistic concurrency) to "real pits of letting AI change code." From a tool or interface you've written that's called by unreliable input, discuss whether "short core logic, long guardrails" is the norm. How would you judge "which guardrails are worth adding, which are over-defense"?
  • The lesson stresses edit's rejection messages ("must match exactly," "set replaceAll to true") are "operating guides written for the model"—because the tool's "user" is a model that adjusts per the error message. How is this similar to/different from "error messages written for humans"? Designing error messages for an LLM-called tool, what would you especially mind (actionability, exposing internal details, guiding correct retry)?

Search & exec tools

Last lesson covered the agent's four hands for changing files. But before changing, you must find what to change; many tasks also need to run a command and see results (install deps, run tests, git status). This lesson's three tools are a coding agent's other two basic skills: search and execute. glob (find by filename), grep (search by file content), bash (run shell commands). The first two are "eyes"—quickly locating in the codebase; the last is the "universal hand"—it can do the odd jobs the specialized tools above can't, but for that very reason has the most power and the most guardrails.

This lesson shows an interesting contrast: specialized vs general. glob/grep are specialized tools—making the high-frequency "search" into two fast, stable, bounded narrow interfaces (both backed by ripgrep); bash is the general escape hatch—it has "the host user's filesystem, process, and network authority," near-omnipotent, so opencode wraps it in a whole ring of seatbelts: time-limited, volume-limited, doubly-permissioned, force-terminable. I'll also clear up a common misconception—the bash tool does NOT run via "PTY (pseudo-terminal)"; PTY is separate infrastructure for "interactive terminals," a different thing from the "run a command, get results" bash tool. Clearing this up sharpens your boundary between "batch execution" and "interactive terminal."

🔦 Analogy
Imagine the agent working in a vast code library. glob is "search by title"—"give me all *.test.ts," and whoosh, a list of titles. grep is "search by a phrase inside the books"—"which books contain TODO: fix," flipping through contents to pull out the hits. Both rely on the library's blazing-fast search machine (ripgrep), auto-skipping the shelves you said to ignore (gitignore). And bash hands the agent the keys to the whole workshop—it can run the machines, haul things, make outside calls (full filesystem, process, network authority). Precisely because this key is so heavy, the workshop door posts iron rules: each job has a time limit (force-stop on overtime), the waste it produces has a cap (truncate on overflow), you must register before starting (permission), and there's a master switch (force-cut power to terminate). Specialized tools are like labeled precision instruments; the general bash is like a workshop needing safety protocols—the greater the power, the stricter the guardrails.

glob and grep: two ways to "find"

First lay the three tools side by side, one table for their division of labor and each one's "boundary"—they all fill lesson 36's Config form too:

ToolDoes whatBacked byKey boundary
globfind files by filename patternRipgreplimit result cap
grepsearch by content regex (+include file glob)Ripgreplimit hit cap
bashrun a shell command (full authority)AppProcess/spawntimeout + 1MB bytes + dual permission

First the two search tools. They solve the same big problem—"locate in the codebase"—but with different cuts:

glob · find by name

input {pattern, path?, limit?}, output file list. "Give me files matching src/**/*.ts." Asks "which files are named this."

grep · search by content

input {pattern(regex), path?, include?, limit?}, output hit list. "Which files have useEffect." Asks "which files wrote this."

Both implementations are built on the Ripgrep service—a smart choice. ripgrep (rg) is one of the industry's acknowledged fastest code-search tools, and respects .gitignore by default: it won't search node_modules, dist into the results to pollute them. opencode didn't hand-write filesystem traversal then implement regex matching (slow and error-prone), but stands on ripgrep's giant shoulders, wrapping it into just two narrow tool interfaces. This is the same wisdom as lesson 35's "don't reinvent the wheel, reuse the authoritative upstream"—for "search," something others have perfected, just borrow it.

Another commonality is both carry limit (result cap). Why does search need a cap too? Because a broad pattern (e.g. grep import) may hit thousands, and stuffing them all back into the conversation would instantly blow the model's context window. limit bounds results to a reasonable range, forcing the model to narrow with a more precise pattern or narrower path rather than scooping a whole sea at once. This, lesson 38's file-tool offset/limit paging, and lesson 42's bounded output are the same red thread running through M7: anywhere "volume could be large" must be bounded—because the model consuming the results has a limited and expensive context window.

bash: the universal hand, and its ring of seatbelts

Now the centerpiece bash. Its description has a heavy line—commands run with "the host user's filesystem, process, and network authority." In other words, what bash can do nearly equals what you yourself can do in a terminal. That's its power (the odd jobs the specialized tools can't reach, it can manage) and its danger (an rm -rf runs just the same). So opencode wraps bash in a whole ring of seatbelts:

time-limited
default 2 min, max 10 min (≤ MAX_TIMEOUT_MS at the schema layer); timeout→clear "timed out, retry with a larger timeout"
volume-limited
stdout/stderr each 1 MB in-memory cap (MAX_CAPTURE_BYTES); overflow→truncate + a truncated notice
dual permission
one for workdir (external dir needs external_directory approval) + one for the command itself, permission.assert
force-terminable
detached launch (non-Windows) for group cleanup; forceKillAfter 3s grace then force-end
non-interactive
stdin set to ignore—the command can't turn around and ask the model for input (the model can't answer)

Connect these and a clear design intent emerges: give the strongest capability the strictest constraints. Specialized tools like glob/grep can do limited damage, with light guardrails; the "do-anything" escape hatch bash is hemmed in time-limited, volume-limited, place-limited, interruptible. Among these, the time limit is especially crucial—an npm install may take tens of seconds, but a mistakenly-written infinite loop runs forever; the timeout (default 2 min) leashes every command with a time belt, force-stopping on overrun, never letting one command hang the whole agent. The post-timeout handling is thoughtful too: not a dry error but "timed out; if this command was meant to run longer, retry with a larger timeout"—again an actionable guide written for the model (echoing lesson 38). The main execution flow is a chain of "resolve→permit→launch→run-with-limit→collect":

resolve workdir
relative to Location; external needs approval
permission.assert →
spawn child process
detached + configured shell
run(timeout, maxBytes) →
run time+volume bounded
timeout→timedOut; overflow→truncated
→
{exitCode, output, truncated…}
structured result back

The same chain lands in several different outcomes, and output faithfully encodes them all into the structured result, so the model sees at a glance "did it succeed, err, hang, or get truncated":

successexitCode=0, output is the command's full output
nonzero exitexitCode≠0 (the command errored), output includes stderr—the model judges the cause
timeouttimedOut=true, output="timed out, retry with a larger timeout"
output overflowtruncated=true, output truncated + a "capture truncated" notice

Clarification: the bash tool ≠ a PTY terminal

Here we must dispel a common confusion. You may have heard opencode has "PTY / pseudo-terminal," and guessed the bash tool runs via PTY. Actually it doesn't. Look at the source: the bash tool executes commands via AppProcess.run, and AppProcess underneath is ChildProcessSpawner (a cross-platform wrapper over child_process/spawn)—it's a batch model of "launch a child process, run to completion, collect stdout/stderr," not a pseudo-terminal. So what's the pty/* module for? It's a different thing: pseudo-terminal infrastructure for "interactive terminals" (remember lesson 10's pty/pty-connect route groups, lesson 33's mention that WebSocketTracker tracks pty terminals? That's for opening a live, typeable shell session in the UI). The difference is essential:

bash tool (batch)

spawn a child process → run one command → collect stdout/stderr → done. One round trip, non-interactive, for the model.

pty module (interactive)

A persistent pseudo-terminal: onData streams output, write feeds input, resize adjusts size. For a human opening a live shell.

Why does the bash tool not use PTY? Because the model needs the deterministic batch of "run one command, get the complete result," not a live stream that keeps spitting characters and may wait for your input. PTY's "streaming + typeable" is actually a burden for a model that can't be interactively prompted (which is exactly why stdin is set to ignore). Splitting "run commands for the model" and "open a terminal for a human" into two different mechanisms, each serving its own interaction model—a clear-headed division. Next time you see pty in opencode, you'll know: that's the terminal feature, not the bash tool.

Following this "different mechanism for different interaction model" one layer further: batch suits the model because the model's "think—act" rhythm is itself question-and-answer—it sends one command, awaits one complete result, then decides the next, exactly matching "spawn, run, collect." PTY's value is real-time and interactivity: a human watching the terminal wants to see characters spat out one by one, to press Ctrl-C anytime, to type y when the program asks "confirm?". These two needs are essentially different; forcing one mechanism to serve the other only awkwards both. opencode didn't lazily "make the model go through PTY too," but honestly fits a mechanism to each interaction—this "don't force unification" restraint is the same maturity as lesson 34's "different vendors' cache mechanisms, don't pretend they're the same."

🗺️ The Big Picture

Search and execute round out a coding agent's two basic skills beyond "change":

  • glob / grep = two ways to "find": glob by filename, grep by content (regex); both built on Ripgrep (fast, respects .gitignore by default), both carry limit to keep results from blowing the context. Reuse the authoritative upstream (like lesson 35).
  • bash = the general escape hatch: holds the host user's file/process/network authority, does the odd jobs specialized tools can't reach, hence the strictest guardrails—time-limited (default 2 min/max 10 min), volume-limited (stdout/stderr each 1 MB), dual permission (workdir + command), force-terminable (detached + forceKillAfter 3s), non-interactive stdin.
  • Greater power, stricter guardrails: specialized tools do limited damage, light guardrails; general bash is hemmed in time/volume/place-limited and interruptible. Timeout returns an actionable guide like "retry with a larger timeout" (written for the model).
  • bash tool ≠ PTY: bash runs commands via AppProcess.run (spawn/child_process batch); pty/* is separate infrastructure for interactive terminals (live stream + typeable). Two interaction models, two mechanisms.

By here, the coding agent's three basic skills—change (lesson 38), find (glob/grep), run (bash)—form a system. The next lesson (40) wraps up the remaining built-in tools: webfetch (fetch web pages), websearch (web search), question (ask the user back), todowrite (write todos)—extending the agent's "hands" from the local codebase to the network and people. And this lesson's recurring "bound volume, bound time" is pushed to its extreme in lesson 42's "bounded tool output."

🔬 Source Detail

bash's "bounded run + timeout handling" is its core guardrail (simplified from bash.ts):

// spawn opts: detached for group cleanup; 3s grace before force-end on timeout
spawn(command, { cwd, shell, stdin: "ignore",
  detached: process.platform !== "win32", forceKillAfter: Duration.seconds(3) })

const result = yield* appProcess.run(command, {
  timeout: Duration.millis(timeout),       // default 2 min, max 10 min
  maxOutputBytes: MAX_CAPTURE_BYTES,         // stdout cap 1 MB
  maxErrorBytes: MAX_CAPTURE_BYTES,          // stderr cap 1 MB
}).pipe(Effect.catchTag("AppProcessError",
  e => isTimeout(e) ? Effect.succeed(undefined) : Effect.fail(e)))
if (!result) return { ..., output: "Command exceeded timeout ... Retry with a larger timeout", timedOut: true }

Three details show craft. One, timeout is "demoted" to a normal result, not an exception: catchTag turns "timeout," one kind of AppProcessError, into undefined (then a structured result with timedOut:true), while other errors truly fail—timeout is an expected thing, not to be treated as a crash. Two, the volume cap is an "in-memory safety limit": the 1 MB maxOutputBytes guards against a cat huge-file blowing memory; after truncation it clearly marks truncated with a note. Three, timeout is capped at the schema layer by isLessThanOrEqualTo(MAX_TIMEOUT_MS)—even if the model tries to pass a 24-hour timeout, it's stopped at the param-validation gate (lesson 36), never reaching execution. Guardrails aren't only at runtime but at the type/schema layer too; the earlier the interception, the better.

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • glob / grep (tool/{glob,grep}.ts) = two ways to "find": glob by filename pattern, grep by content regex (+include file glob); output file/hit lists. Both built on the Ripgrep service (fast, respects .gitignore by default), both carry limit to keep results from blowing the context. Reuse the authoritative upstream rather than hand-writing traversal.
  • bash (tool/bash.ts) = the general escape hatch: input {command, workdir?, timeout?, description?}, holds host user's file/process/network authority. Guardrails: time-limited (DEFAULT 2 min / MAX 10 min, schema-capped), volume-limited (stdout/stderr each MAX_CAPTURE_BYTES=1 MB), dual permission.assert (workdir + command), detached + forceKillAfter 3s, stdin ignore.
  • Greater power, stricter guardrails: specialized tools light, general bash hemmed in time/volume/place-limited and interruptible. Timeout demoted to a normal result with timedOut:true (catchTag), returning an actionable "retry with a larger timeout" guide.
  • bash ≠ PTY: bash runs commands via AppProcess.run → ChildProcessSpawner (spawn/child_process batch); pty/* (Proc.onData/write/resize) is separate infrastructure for interactive terminals (live stream + typeable, lesson 10's pty routes, lesson 33's WebSocketTracker). Batch vs interactive, two mechanisms.
  • The red thread through M7: anywhere "volume could be large" is bounded—search's limit, file-read's offset/limit, bash's timeout/byte cap, all foreshadowing lesson 42's "bounded tool output." Guardrails start at the schema layer (the earlier the interception, the better).

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What are the two search tools glob and grep built on, and why?
  1. Each hand-writes filesystem traversal and regex matching
  2. Call a cloud search API
  3. Both built on the Ripgrep (rg) service — one of the fastest code searches, respects .gitignore by default; opencode reuses the authoritative upstream rather than hand-writing traversal+regex
  4. Use a database full-text index
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. glob (by filename) and grep (by content regex) are both built on the Ripgrep service. ripgrep is one of the acknowledged fastest code searches, respecting .gitignore by default (not polluting results with node_modules/dist). opencode didn't hand-write traversal+regex (slow, error-prone) but stands on ripgrep's shoulders, wrapping just two narrow interfaces — the same wisdom as lesson 35's "don't reinvent the wheel, reuse the authoritative upstream." Both carry limit to keep results from blowing the model's context.
2. The bash tool holds "the host user's full file/process/network authority"; what guardrails does opencode give it?
  1. Every command needs manual user confirmation
  2. Only allows pre-whitelisted commands
  3. Time-limited (default 2min/max 10min, schema-capped) + volume-limited (stdout/stderr each 1MB) + dual permission (workdir+command) + force-terminable (detached+forceKillAfter 3s) + stdin ignore
  4. No guardrails, runs whatever the model says
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Greater power, stricter guardrails. bash is the "do-anything" escape hatch, hemmed in time/volume/place-limited and interruptible: timeout (default 2min, max 10min, capped ≤MAX_TIMEOUT_MS at the schema layer) prevents an infinite loop hanging the agent; the 1MB in-memory cap prevents cat huge-file blowing memory; dual permission.assert on workdir+command; detached launch + forceKillAfter 3s grace for force cleanup; stdin=ignore prevents commands prompting the model. Timeout is also demoted to a normal result with timedOut, returning an actionable "retry with a larger timeout" guide.
3. The lesson specially clarifies: does the bash tool run via "PTY pseudo-terminal"?
  1. No. bash runs via AppProcess.run→ChildProcessSpawner (spawn/child_process batch); pty/* is separate infrastructure for interactive terminals (live stream + typeable)
  2. bash and pty are the same module
  3. Yes, bash is PTY
  4. bash uses neither spawn nor pty
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. A common misconception. The bash tool executes via AppProcess.run, underneath ChildProcessSpawner (a cross-platform wrapper over child_process/spawn) — a batch model of "launch child process, run, collect stdout/stderr," not a pseudo-terminal. pty/* (Proc.onData/write/resize) is separate: for interactive terminals (lesson 10's pty/pty-connect routes, lesson 33's WebSocketTracker), letting a human open a live typeable shell in the UI. The model needs deterministic batch (Q&A), not a live stream that spits characters and awaits input (so stdin is ignore). Two interaction models, two mechanisms, an honest split rather than forced unification.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson splits tools into "specialized" (glob/grep, making high-frequency things narrow and fast) and "general" (bash, the full-authority escape hatch), with the principle "greater power, stricter guardrails." From a system you've designed or used, discuss this principle's manifestations (sudo, double-confirmation for dangerous APIs, write permission in production). What are the pros/cons of one "do-anything" general interface versus a set of "each minds its own" specialized interfaces?
  • The lesson stresses opencode uses two different mechanisms for "run commands for the model" (batch spawn) and "open a terminal for a human" (PTY streaming, typeable), refusing to force one onto the other interaction. Argue: why are "real-time interactive" and "deterministic batch" essentially different needs? Contrasting the model's "think-act" rhythm (Q&A) with a human watching a terminal (wants real-time, Ctrl-C, typing y), explain why each needs a different execution model.

Other built-in tools

The prior two lessons' tools all worked in the agent's own workshop—changing local files, searching local code, running local commands. But a truly useful coding agent can't be a worker sealed in a room. It must be able to look outward (check the latest docs online, search for info past its knowledge cutoff), ask for help (ask the user back when unsure), and take notes for itself (write todos in multi-step tasks, track progress). This lesson's four tools extend the agent's "hands" from the local codebase to the network and to people: webfetch (fetch web pages), websearch (web search), question (ask the user back), todowrite (write todos).

They look miscellaneous but unfold along two new axes. One is outward, toward the network: webfetch lets the agent read a specific web page, websearch lets it search the whole internet—breaking through the two walls of "only knows local code" and "knowledge frozen at training cutoff." The other is turning toward people and self: question is the rare "reverse" tool—all other tools push the agent's will outward (change files, run commands), but it pulls info back from a person; todowrite is even more special, acting not on the external world but on the agent's own state (a todo list), a piece of "externalized working memory." Grasp these two axes and you'll understand how an agent grows from "head-down worker" into a collaborator who can search, ask, and plan.

🪟 Analogy
If the prior lessons' tools are the agent's hands in its own workshop, these four open three new things for that workshop. webfetch is like a window you can aim—"let me see what's behind that URL," bringing the outside view (a web page) in. websearch is like a phone to ask the librarian—"any recent news on X?", asking about the present, past the training cutoff. question is like an intercom on the wall—when unsure, when you shouldn't decide alone, press it and ask "boss, A or B for this step?". todowrite is a personal to-do whiteboard—for multi-step work, write the plan up, check off as you go, so you and the boss can see progress at a glance. From "only head-down working" to "can look out, can speak up, can write down"—these four turn a worker into a reliable collaborator.

Four tools: two new axes

First lay the four side by side, noting they still fill lesson 36's Config form, only the "hand" reaches a new direction:

ToolDoes whatReaches towardinput gist
webfetchfetch a URL's contentnetwork (specific page)url, format(default markdown), timeout?
websearchsearch the internetnetwork (open search)query, numResults?(default 8), type?
questionask the user, get answers backpeople (pull info back)questions[] (multiple choice)
todowritewrite/update the session todo listself (externalized memory)todos[] (with status)

This table's "reaches toward" column is this lesson's skeleton. Almost all prior tools pointed at the "local filesystem"; these four split the direction into three brand-new destinations—network, people, the agent itself. An agent's capability boundary is largely set by "where its hands can reach": an agent that can only change local files is stumped by "how do I use this library's latest API"; an agent that can webfetch / websearch can look it up on the fly. Extending capability is essentially extending "the world the hands can touch." And because the directions differ, the "trust and risk" these four trigger also differ: reading the network must guard against content poisoning, asking the user interrupts them, writing todos only touches the self—classifying them by "reaches toward" isn't just mnemonic but hints at what boundary and permission each deserves (lesson 41 picks up this thread).

Outward · toward the network

webfetch (read a specific page) + websearch (search the open net). Breaking the "only knows local" and "knowledge cutoff" walls, letting the agent look up on the fly.

Inward · people and self

question (pull a decision from a person) + todowrite (write its own todos). Letting the agent ask for help and plan, rather than tough it out head-down.

Outward: webfetch and websearch

First the two reaching toward the network. webfetch fetches an HTTP(S) URL's content, with a noteworthy default: format defaults to markdown (text / html optional). Why not default to raw HTML? Because a page's raw HTML is stuffed with tags, scripts, styles—both token-costly and hard to read for the model. webfetch by default converts the page to clean markdown before giving it to the model, the body crisp. This is again the consistent "plain language for the model" thinking (lesson 36's content side): not dumping the external world raw to the model, but translating it into the form the model digests best. It carries a timeout cap too—fetching a slow site shouldn't leave the agent waiting. The three formats each have their use:

markdown (default)
HTML→clean markdown, crisp body, token-saving, best for the model to read
text
plain text, not even markdown marks, just the words
html
raw HTML, for when the model really needs to see the tag structure

websearch searches the whole internet, specially treating the "past knowledge cutoff" problem. Hidden here is an easy-to-miss but important architectural distinction:

local websearch (this lesson's)

opencode's own, provider-independent local tool, backed by Exa or Parallel search services. Executes on opencode's side.

provider-hosted web search

A different thing: provided by the model provider, executing on the provider's side (route-owned, recall lesson 30's Anthropic server_tool).

In other words, "letting the model search the web" actually has two paths: one is opencode taking an Exa/Parallel key and searching locally (this websearch tool), the other is using the provider's built-in search (running on the provider's server). opencode makes the local path a standard tool, so "web-search capability" doesn't depend on "whether your model provider supports it"—again a design of "sink the capability to your own layer, not bound by the upstream" (echoing lesson 28's "not held hostage by the provider"). The benefit is concrete: whether you use Anthropic today or switch to Gemini tomorrow, web search's behavior stays consistent, not flickering on/off or strong/weak with the provider switch; and opencode can independently decide which search backend (Exa / Parallel) to use and how to tune it, keeping the initiative in its own hands. One more thoughtful detail: websearch's description injects "the current year", reminding the model "include this year when searching for recent info"—because the model's own "today" is frozen at the training cutoff, and without being told the real year, it may search with a stale year. A one-line injection fills in the model's "sense of time."

Inward: question and todowrite

The other two tools turn inward. question is the oddball of the whole toolset—the vast majority of tools push the agent's will outward (I want to change this file, run this command), but question goes the reverse: the agent proactively throws a question to the user and awaits an answer. Its reason for being is to teach the agent to ask when it should, rather than deciding alone before ambiguity and major choices. Its design shows care for collaboration UX too: it encourages multiple choice (fastest for the user to click), and the description explicitly tells the model not to add its own "Other" option—because when custom is on (default), the UI auto-adds a "type your own answer." This "don't add Other, let the UI add it" convention avoids the model and UI each adding one, duplicate and awkward. Listing question's UX conventions shows its care for "human-machine collaboration":

multiple choice first
give options for the user to click where possible, faster than typing, easier too
UI auto-adds freeform
custom on by default → UI auto-adds "type your own answer"
don't add Other
the model shouldn't stuff its own "Other," lest it duplicate the UI's freeform

todowrite is more philosophical: a tool acting on the agent's own state. It changes nothing in the external world, only maintaining "a structured todo list for the current session" (backed by the SessionTodo service). In multi-step tasks the model uses it to write down the plan, updating statuses as it goes. Think of it as a piece of "externalized working memory":

write the plan
multi-step task starts, list the steps as todos (each with status)
track progress
after each step, update the corresponding todo's status (pending→done…)
don't forget
in long tasks, anchor "what's left" via this list, visible to model and user

Why does an agent need a "write todos" tool? Because in a long task of a dozen-plus steps, the model easily forgets the big picture mid-work—its attention is consumed by the current step, possibly missing what's due later. todowrite moves "plan and progress" from the model's volatile mind to a persistent, visible list, helping the model stay on track and letting the user see in real time "what it plans to do, where it's gotten." This is essentially making the humble wisdom of "list it, check off as you go" into an agent tool. An agent that lists todos and tracks progress is far more reliable than one that only dives in head-first. Deeper still, todowrite externalizes the agent's "thinking" into an observable artifact: the user needn't guess "how exactly does it plan to do this," but looks straight at that list—this transparency is itself the basis of trust. An agent that lays its plan in the open, checking off step by step, makes people dare to hand it more complex work.

🗺️ The Big Picture

These four tools push the agent's capability boundary far beyond "the local codebase" along two new axes:

  • Outward · network: webfetch (fetch a specific URL, default-converts to markdown for the model, with timeout) + websearch (search the open net, breaking the knowledge cutoff; a local Exa/Parallel tool, distinct from the provider's own server-side search; description injects the current year).
  • Inward · people and self: question (a reverse tool, pulling answers from the user; multiple-choice first, UI auto-adds freeform, don't add Other) + todowrite (acts on the agent's own state, maintaining session todos, an "externalized working memory").
  • Still the same Config form: each tool declares input/output schema + execute, wrapped in permission—again confirming lesson 36's "one form, each fills blanks," only the "hand" reaches toward network, people, self.
  • Capability = the world the hands can touch: an agent that can only change local files is stumped by "how to use the latest API"; an agent that can search, ask, and plan is a reliable collaborator.

By here, opencode's built-in tool family is complete: file (read/write/edit/apply-patch), search & exec (glob/grep/bash), network & collaboration (webfetch/websearch/question/todowrite), plus lesson 43's skill later. The next lesson (41) turns back to a cross-cutting line running through all tools—the permission system: the withPermission / permission.assert appearing in every tool above—how it evaluates, and how it hands the "may this step be done" decision back to the user. Tools cover "what can be done," permissions cover "what's allowed"—the former is capability, the latter the boundary; together they make an agent both useful and controllable.

🔬 Source Detail

webfetch's format default uses an elegant schema idiom (simplified from webfetch.ts):

// format three-way; on decode, the default fills "markdown" automatically
format: Schema.Literals(["text", "markdown", "html"])
  .pipe(Schema.withDecodingDefault(() => "markdown"))

// websearch: write the current year into the model-facing description
description = `...The current year is ${'$'}{new Date().getFullYear()}. Use this year when searching for recent information...`

Both embody "write the care for the model into the definition." withDecodingDefault lets the model omit format—omit it and it's markdown, sparing the model the burden of thinking "which format do I want" each time, defaulting to the most digestible. And websearch splicing new Date().getFullYear() into the description dynamically computes the real year each time the tool definition is built—the model's training knowledge has a "time blind spot," it doesn't know "what year it is now," and this one injection fills that blind spot. These details change nothing about "what the tool can do," yet markedly improve "how well the model uses it"—a good tool gives the model not just capability but also thinks ahead about "how to use it well" for the model. This is the whole set of thoughtfulness born from the premise "the tool's user is a model."

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • Four tools push the agent's capability along two new axes (tool/{webfetch,websearch,question,todowrite}.ts), all different fillings of lesson 36's Config form.
  • webfetch: fetch an HTTP(S) URL, format defaults to markdown (text/html optional)—converting the page to model-digestible clean body, not raw HTML; carries a timeout cap.
  • websearch: search the open net, breaking the "knowledge cutoff." It's opencode's local tool (backed by Exa/Parallel), distinct from "provider's own, executing provider-side" web search—"web-search capability" not bound to the model provider. The description injects the current year to fill the model's time blind spot.
  • question: the rare reverse tool, pulling answers from the user (handling ambiguity/major choices). Multiple-choice first; with custom on by default the UI auto-adds freeform, so it tells the model not to add "Other."
  • todowrite: acts on the agent's own state (backed by SessionTodo), maintaining session todos, updating status as it goes—an "externalized working memory" helping the model in long tasks not forget the big picture and letting the user see progress. Can-search/ask/plan is the mark of a reliable collaborator.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What do webfetch/websearch/question/todowrite add compared to the earlier file/search/exec tools?
  1. They replace the file tools
  2. Extend the agent's "hands" from the local codebase to three new destinations: the network (webfetch/websearch), people (question), the agent's own state (todowrite)
  3. They're not tools but config options
  4. Just aliases of the earlier tools
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Earlier tools almost all pointed at the local filesystem; these four push along two new axes: outward to the network (webfetch reads a specific page, websearch searches the open net, breaking the "only local" and "knowledge cutoff" walls), inward to people and self (question pulls answers from the user, todowrite maintains the agent's own todos). An agent's capability boundary is largely set by "where its hands can reach." All four are still different fillings of lesson 36's Config form.
2. How does opencode's websearch tool differ from "the provider's own web search," and why does opencode make a local one?
  1. The provider's doesn't exist
  2. The local one is slower but cheaper
  3. This websearch is opencode's own local tool (backed by Exa/Parallel), executing on opencode's side; the provider's executes provider-side. Making its own → web-search capability isn't bound to the model provider, behavior consistent across provider switches
  4. They're identical
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. "Letting the model search the web" has two paths: opencode searching locally with an Exa/Parallel key (this websearch), or using the provider's built-in search (running on the provider's server). opencode makes the local path a standard tool, so web-search capability doesn't depend on "whether your provider supports it" — Anthropic today or Gemini tomorrow, behavior stays consistent, and opencode independently decides which backend and tuning. This is "sink the capability to your own layer, not bound by the upstream" (echoing lesson 28). The description also injects the current year to fill the model's time blind spot.
3. What's special about todowrite's "target of effect"?
  1. It changes nothing external, only maintaining the agent's own session todo list (SessionTodo) — an "externalized working memory," helping the model in long tasks not forget the big picture and letting the user see progress
  2. It makes network requests
  3. It directly modifies the user's filesystem
  4. It restarts the agent
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Most tools push the will outward (change files, run commands, search); todowrite acts on the agent's own state — maintaining "the current session's structured todos" (backed by SessionTodo). In a dozen-plus-step long task, the model's attention is consumed by the current step and easily forgets the big picture; todowrite moves plan and progress from the model's volatile mind to a persistent, visible list, helping the model stay on track and letting the user see in real time "what it plans, where it's at." It also externalizes the agent's thinking into an observable artifact, and this transparency is the basis of trust.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson calls question the "oddball" of the toolset—other tools push the agent's will outward, but it pulls info back from a person. Discuss how an agent that "asks when it should" beats one that "always decides alone." Also: what decisions should be asked of the user, which decided alone? What are the harms of over-asking (asking everything) versus under-asking (deciding major choices unilaterally)?
  • A recurring theme: the tool's "user" is a model, so the definitions hide care for the model everywhere—webfetch defaults to markdown, websearch injects the current year, format is omittable defaulting to markdown. Give another interface detail where "designed for LLM calls" vs "designed for human calls" would make a different tradeoff (return format, defaults, error messages, field naming). Designing an interface for a model, what would you especially optimize?

Permissions

Over five lessons we equipped the agent with a powerful pair of hands: changing files, running commands, searching the web, fetching pages, asking people. But the greater the power, the more it needs a leash—an agent that can rm -rf, push code live, send outbound requests, if unmanaged, is a disaster not an assistant. This lesson's permission system is that leash. It answers a question running through all tools: this step—is it actually allowed? You may have noticed every prior tool's execute hides a permission.assert(...)—they all lead here. Tools cover "what can be done," permissions cover "what's allowed"; the former is capability, the latter the boundary, and together they make an agent both useful and controllable.

This system's core is surprisingly clean—it's essentially an allow / deny / ask three-state rule engine: each rule says "for some action on some resource, permit / deny / ask"; evaluation is last-match-wins, and when no rule matches, the default is "ask." This "default-ask" design is the soul of the whole security model: for the agent to do anything not pre-approved and sensitive, it must either hit an allow rule or stop and await the user's nod. Better still, the user's reply has three levels—once / always (and remember it) / reject—where always persists the decision into a rule, so the system asks less and less as trust accumulates. Grasp this lesson and you'll understand how opencode finds the dynamic balance point between "let the agent run free" and "keep a human in the loop."

🛂 Analogy
Picture the permission system as a building's security desk + a rulebook. Every time a worker (the agent) wants to enter a room to do something sensitive (run some tool on some resource), they badge at the desk. The desk flips the rulebook: marked "allow" → wave through; marked "deny" → blocked (and such rooms don't even appear on the worker's floor map); not mentioned in the rulebook → the desk buzzes the manager (user) to decide. The manager can say three things: "just this once" (let this through), "always" (let through, and the desk writes it into the rulebook so it won't bother the manager next time), "no" (blocked, possibly with a "why not" passed to the worker). Over time, the rulebook fills with the manager's standing decisions, and the desk buzzes less and less—the worker grows handier while the manager keeps final say over "new situations not in the book." Run free, but not out of control—that's the security desk's wisdom.

The three-state rule engine: allow / deny / ask

The permission system's core is a strikingly humble data structure. Open permission/schema.ts, and a rule is just three fields:

// permission/schema.ts —— one rule
Rule = { action: string, resource: string, effect: "allow" | "deny" | "ask" }
Ruleset = Rule[]

action (do what, e.g. edit/bash), resource (on whom, e.g. a file path), effect (what to do). The three effects are the system's entire "stance":

allow
permit, execute silently, don't bother the user
deny
refuse, throw DeniedError; and such a tool is kept off the menu by lesson 37's "whollyDisabled"
ask
ask the user—emit a permission.v2.asked event, stop and await an answer

Evaluation relies on an evaluate(action, resource, ...rulesets) function, just two lines of logic, every word crucial: concatenate the rulesets, findLast the last rule whose action and resource both wildcard-match, use its effect; if none match, default to effect: "ask". These two lines define the whole security model's two iron laws:

Last match wins (findLast)

Rules in order, later takes precedence. You can write a broad rule then override with a specific one—same approach as a firewall ruleset.

Default is "ask"

An action no rule mentions defaults to neither allow nor deny, but ask the human. This is "secure by default": unknown = leave it to a person.

This "default-ask," seemingly ordinary, is actually the design's keystone. It means: the agent can't quietly accomplish something unapproved by "gaming the rules"—any sensitive operation the rulebook didn't explicitly permit triggers a user prompt. New tools, new scenarios, edge cases… everything "not pre-considered" falls into the "ask" safety net. Defaulting "unknown" to "ask a human" rather than "allow" is this permission system's single most important choice. Reverse it to see the weight: if the default were "allow," then every new action the designers didn't foresee could let the agent silently do something you'd have wanted to block; defaulting to "ask" plugs this "slip-through" risk at the root—at the mere cost of a few extra prompts early on. Between "safety" and "convenience," this system unhesitatingly picks the former, then earns "convenience" back bit by bit via always.

Evaluation flow: one complete adjudication by assert

How does this ruleset act on a concrete tool call? Look at permission.assert—that line in every prior tool's execute. It launches a complete adjudication:

tool calls assert
{action, resource}
evaluate →
check effective ruleset
findLast, unmatched→ask
three-way branch →
allow→proceed
deny→DeniedError
ask→prompt, await

Three branches: deny → throw DeniedError outright, the tool can't run at all; allow → return silently, the tool runs; ask → emit a permission.v2.asked event and suspend (a Deferred pauses this execution in place) until the user answers. Note the whole assert is wrapped in uninterruptibleMask—the "ask—await—answer" sequence can't be interrupted midway, else you'd get "asked halfway, state scrambled." Again lesson 8's "atomize critical timing."

Worth pausing here to see that deny and ask actually guard two different gates—a kind of defense in depth:

deny · menu layer (lesson 37)

A wholly-denied tool is filtered out by whollyDisabled at materialize, not even appearing in the list sent to the model—the model doesn't even know "this tool exists."

ask · call layer (this lesson)

The tool is on the menu, the model can pick it, but each time it's actually about to execute, assert asks first—the last gate before execution.

The two levels' division is careful: "never allowed" things are made invisible to the model at the source (denying it even the chance to "think about it," sparing the explain-and-refuse round trips); "depends" things stay on the menu, asked at the last moment. The cleanest refusal is to not let the other know the option exists (echoing lesson 37), the most flexible gatekeeping is asking consent on the spot—deny and ask each do their job, one governing "can it exist," the other "is this time OK."

The user's reply has three levels, each corresponding to a different "future":

oncepermit just this once; the same thing next time still asks
alwayspermit + write into a persisted rule (saved.add → SQLite, project-scoped); future same thing is allowed directly, no more asking
rejectrefuse this time → RejectedError; with feedback → CorrectedError (carry "why not" back to the model)

The two forms of reject deserve a closer look. Just saying "no" (RejectedError) is pure interception; but if the user attaches a reason when rejecting, it becomes CorrectedError—and that reason flows back to the model with the error. This stroke is clever: it turns "rejection" from a dead end into a course-correction. The user needn't just "block" but can "block and tell it how to fix"—e.g. "don't touch the prod config, change the staging one." Receiving this feedback, the model can adjust and retry, rather than slamming into a wall, at a loss. So the permission system isn't just a "gate" but a loop feeding human judgment back to the agent: a human in the loop isn't only "stop the dangerous" but "steer the agent toward right."

always: a system that bothers you less as trust grows

The always level is the design's most "humane" stroke. It does more than "permit this time"; it solidifies the user's decision into a rule, stored in the database (PermissionSaved → the permission table, with a unique index on project_id + action + resource, project-scoped). Next time the same "action × resource" comes up for evaluation, this saved rule makes evaluate return allow directly—no more bothering the user.

user answers always
for some action×resource
saved.add →
write to permission table
project-scoped, survives across sessions
next evaluate →
match this rule → allow
silently permit, no more asking

This "always persists" design gives the whole permission system a learning feel: at first, the agent asks you about everything, a bit naggy; but every time you click "always," you shed a class of future interruptions. The longer you use it, the more trust accumulates, the less the system asks, approaching "what you're comfortable letting it do automatically, it really does automatically; what you care about, it still asks first." It's a dynamic balance: conservative at first (default-ask), gradually loosening with user authorization (always accrues rules), handing the decision of "how much freedom" to the user bit by bit, revocably—rather than forcing an awkward up-front choice between "fully open" and "ask about everything." The source has a thoughtful detail too: when an always rule is saved, it incidentally permits other pending asks that the new rule now covers—you approve once, and related waits all release together.

🗺️ The Big Picture

The permission system is the cross-cutting security layer through the whole tool system, fitting "capability" with a "boundary":

  • Three-state rule engine (permission/schema.ts): Rule = {action, resource, effect: allow|deny|ask}. evaluate concatenates rulesets, findLast wildcard-match (last match wins), unmatched defaults to ask (secure default: unknown = leave to a human).
  • assert adjudication: tool permission.assert → evaluate → deny throws DeniedError / allow proceeds silently / ask emits permission.v2.asked and suspends awaiting (wrapped in uninterruptibleMask for atomicity).
  • Three replies once/always/reject: once just this time; always permits + persists a rule (saved.add → SQLite permission table, project-scoped); reject → RejectedError (with feedback → CorrectedError, the reason back to the model).
  • Two gating levels: deny at the "menu layer" (lesson 37 whollyDisabled, the tool not even shown to the model); ask at the "call layer" (prompt before executing). Defense in depth.
  • Bothers less as trust grows: conservative by default (ask), gradually loosens after always accrues rules—dynamically balancing "run free" and "human in the loop."

With permissions as the leash, M7's tool system has its "capability + boundary" complete. The next lesson (42) returns to another red thread through M7—bounded tool output: a grep/bash may spit tens of thousands of lines; how to truncate, how to spill the full text to a managed file, without blowing the model's context yet without losing info. Lesson 43 then covers the Skills system, wrapping up M7. Permissions govern "may it be done," bounded output governs "how the finished result is safely handed back to the model"—both key pieces making a powerful agent truly controllable, trustworthy, and worth entrusting.

🔬 Source Detail

The whole engine's core is this dozen-line evaluate (simplified from permission.ts):

// last match wins; if none match → default ask
function evaluate(action, resource, ...rulesets) {
  return rulesets.flat()
    .findLast(rule =>
      Wildcard.match(action, rule.action) && Wildcard.match(resource, rule.resource))
    ?? { action, resource, effect: "ask" }   // ← secure default
}

Three details show craft. One, findLast not find—later-defined rules override earlier, so the natural expression "broad rule as base + narrow rule as exception" holds (saved rules, agent rules, config rules concatenated, order = precedence). Two, that ?? { effect: "ask" } fallback—catching "no rule covers it," the most common case, squarely onto "ask a human," not silently allowing. Three, Wildcard.match lets rules use *—{action:"*", resource:"*", effect:"deny"} is a "deny-all" fallback (missingAgentPermissions for agents lacking permission is exactly this). A dozen lines carry "secure default, stackable override, wildcardable" three properties—a good security mechanism often relies not on complexity but on putting the right default in the right place.

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • Permission is the cross-cutting layer through all tools (core/src/permission.ts + permission/{schema,saved,sql}.ts): every prior tool's permission.assert leads here. Tools = "what can be done," permissions = "what's allowed."
  • Three-state rule engine: Rule={action, resource, effect: allow|deny|ask}. evaluate = concatenate rulesets then findLast wildcard-match (last match wins, order = precedence), unmatched defaults to ask (secure default: unknown left to a human, the agent can't game it into doing unapproved things silently).
  • assert adjudication: deny→DeniedError (and lesson 37's whollyDisabled keeps it off the menu); allow→proceed silently; ask→emit permission.v2.asked event, suspend awaiting within uninterruptibleMask.
  • Replies once/always/reject: once just this time; always→permit + persist a rule (saved.add→SQLite permission table, project-scoped, cross-session, next time allowed directly, and incidentally releasing other pending asks the new rule covers); reject→RejectedError/with-feedback CorrectedError (reason back to the model = steering, not just blocking).
  • Two gating levels + learning: deny at the menu layer, ask at the call layer (defense in depth); conservative by default (ask), gradually loosening after always accrues rules—dynamically balancing "run free" and "human in the loop," handing the freedom decision revocably to the user.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. When no rule matches, what effect does the permission system's evaluate default to, and why is this default crucial?
  1. Crashes outright
  2. Defaults to allow, whatever's convenient
  3. Defaults to ask (ask the user) — secure default: every "not pre-considered" sensitive operation falls into the "ask" safety net, the agent can't game it into doing unapproved things silently
  4. Defaults to deny, refuse everything
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. evaluate concatenates rulesets, findLast wildcard-match (last match wins), and when none match defaults to effect:ask. This is the security model's keystone: if it defaulted to allow, every new action the designers didn't foresee could let the agent silently do something you'd want blocked; defaulting to ask plugs this slip-through at the root, at the cost of a few early prompts, earning convenience back via always. Defaulting "unknown" to "ask" rather than "allow" is the system's most important choice.
2. What happens when the user answers always to an ask?
  1. Permanently disables the tool
  2. Permit this time + persist the decision into a rule (saved.add→SQLite permission table, project-scoped), future same action×resource is allowed directly, no more asking; also releases other pending asks the new rule covers
  3. Does nothing
  4. Permits just this once, asks again next time
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. always does more than "permit this time"; it solidifies the user's decision into a rule stored in the permission table (project-scoped, cross-session). Next time the same action×resource is evaluated, this saved rule makes evaluate return allow directly, no more bothering the user. This gives the system a "learning" feel: conservative by default (ask), each always sheds a class of future interruptions, asking less as trust accumulates — dynamically balancing "run free" and "human in the loop," handing the freedom decision revocably to the user. The source also releases other pending asks the new rule covers.
3. At which layer do the two effects deny and ask each gate?
  1. Both only at the database layer
  2. Both at the same layer doing the same thing
  3. deny at the "menu layer" (lesson 37 whollyDisabled, the tool not even shown to the model); ask at the "call layer" (the tool's on the menu, the model can pick it, but asks before executing) — defense in depth
  4. deny at the call layer, ask at the menu layer
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Defense in depth: a wholly-denied tool is filtered by whollyDisabled at materialize, not even entering the list sent to the model — the model doesn't even know "this tool exists" (the cleanest refusal is to not let the other know the option exists, lesson 37). An ask tool is on the menu and the model can pick it, but each time it's about to execute, assert asks first — the last gate before execution. deny governs "can it exist," ask governs "is this time OK," each its own job.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says the permission system finds a dynamic balance between "let the agent run free" and "keep a human in the loop": conservative by default (ask), gradually loosening as always accrues rules, handing the freedom decision revocably to the user. Using permission/consent mechanisms you've used (mobile app permissions, sudo, CI deploy approvals), discuss the pros/cons of "one-time consent vs permanent authorization vs ask every time." How should a good "human-in-the-loop" system adjust how often it bothers the user over time?
  • The lesson notes reject has two forms: pure RejectedError (just "no") and CorrectedError (reject + attached reason, the reason fed back to the model to course-correct), the latter turning "rejection" from a dead end into a correction, making "human in the loop" not just block danger but steer the agent toward right. Argue: why is "rejection with feedback" more valuable than "plain rejection" for an LLM agent? Is this the same wisdom as "don't just say no, say how to fix it" when giving feedback to people/systems?

Bounded tool output

This lesson ties off a red thread recurring since M7's start—bounding. We've seen it all along: file-read's offset/limit, search's limit, bash's timeout and byte cap. They all guard the same thing: a tool's output can be large enough to blow the model's context window. A grep import may hit tens of thousands of lines, a cat big-log can dump megabytes—stuffing these raw back into the conversation would instantly fill the model's limited, expensive context, at best burning a fortune in tokens, at worst pushing the genuinely important conversation out of the window. This lesson's ToolOutputStore is the final bounding gate on the tool-execution flow: it ensures no single tool's output can flood the model, without truly losing information.

Its solution has two clever parts. One, truncation isn't "chop off the tail" but "keep both ends, cut the middle"—for logs, test results, file dumps, the most useful info concentrates at the start (what's running) and the end (result/error), the middle being a long repetitive body; so it keeps the first half + last half, glossing the middle with a marker. Two, while truncating, it spills the full text to a managed file and hands the file path to the model too—the model sees "a crisp preview + where the full text is," and if it really needs the whole thing, it reads that file with the read tool. A bounded view, a complete backup, on-demand access—all three together protect the context yet lose not a byte. Grasp this lesson and you'll understand how an agent safely coexists with "tool output that could be infinitely large."

📄 Analogy
Imagine a tool finishes and spits out a five-hundred-page report; but you (the model) can only hold a few pages at once (the context window is just that big). What to do? Hand you all 500—you can't hold a single page, they all scatter; give you just the first five—you miss the final conclusion and errors, which are often the key. ToolOutputStore is like a clever assistant: it gives you the first few pages + the last few pages (the lead-up and the outcome, usually most important), with a slip in between: "…middle omitted, full report filed in drawer #42…." You grasp the gist right away; should you really need the middle, go open that drawer (read the managed file with the read tool). Gist up front, full text stored, retrievable anytime—you're not drowned by 500 pages, yet not a word is lost. This is the assistant's way of "bounded but not distorted."

Why it must be bounded: context is a scarce resource

First, the motivation. The model's context window is finite—what it can "read" at once has a ceiling (tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands of tokens), and every token costs money and time. Yet a tool's output can be boundlessly large:

grep too broad
searching a common word, hitting thousands of lines
cat a big file
a log/data file dumping megabytes
bash verbose
a build/test command spitting masses of progress and stacks

Pouring these raw back into the conversation has a double consequence: one, burning money and slowing things (tens of thousands of lines' token cost and latency), two, more fatal—it pushes the genuinely important out of the window. The context is only so big; filled with a lump of irrelevant logs, the model "forgets" the earlier requirements, the just-made plan. So "bounding" isn't stinginess but necessary protection of a scarce resource. Recall lesson 37: the registry's settle calls resources.bound(...) after a tool executes—that resources is this lesson's ToolOutputStore, the final gate every tool result passes before returning to the model.

Here we must distinguish it from lesson 39's bash byte cap—they're two different bounds, each guarding its own:

bash's 1 MB memory cap (lesson 39)

Guards "memory": a command's stdout can't pile up unboundedly in the process and blow memory. An earlier, lower-level gate.

ToolOutputStore's bound (this lesson)

Guards "context": any tool's result, before returning to the model, is bounded to 2000 lines / 50 KB. The final, model-facing gate.

Cut head and tail: the wisdom of truncation

The core is the bound function and the preview it calls. First the bounds it honors:

MAX_LINES = 2000
line cap; overridable via config's tool_output.max_lines
MAX_BYTES = 50 KB
byte cap; overridable via max_bytes. Lines/bytes dual gating
RETENTION = 7 days
managed-file retention, auto-cleaned on expiry

The default bounds are MAX_LINES = 2000 lines, MAX_BYTES = 50 KB. When output exceeds, preview's truncation strategy is careful—not simply chopping the excess, but keeping half at each end:

over-long output
say 10,000 lines
preview →
first 1000 lines
headLines=⌈max/2⌉
+ marker +
last 1000 lines
tailLines=⌊max/2⌋

Why head + tail, not the most intuitive "first N lines"? Because of insight into tool output's information distribution: a log, a test run, a file dump—the most valuable info almost always sits at the two ends—the start tells you "what's running, what config," the end tells you "the result, any error, the final summary"; the middle is often a long repetitive run of progress/data lines, lowest in info density. Naively "keep only the first 1000 lines" would precisely chop off the all-important 'final result / error'—the model reads progress forever yet doesn't know whether it succeeded. preview keeps the first half, last half, with a marker between (like "…truncated…"), exactly using the same budget to keep the highest-info-density two ends. Same when bytes exceed—head headBytes, tail tailBytes, half each. A seemingly trivial "keep both ends" rests on a real understanding of "what tool output looks like." Think of how you read a very long test log: don't you also first glance at the start "which cases ran," then jump straight to the end "how many passed/failed, where the error is"? The thousands of progress lines in between, mostly skimmed past. preview's "cut head and tail" does exactly this most-human-intuitive thing—it treats truncation not as a brainless "chop to N lines" mechanical op, but as an optimization problem of "keep as much useful info as possible within a limited budget."

Spill and retrieve: the full text isn't lost, readable on demand

Truncation inevitably loses info—what about that big chopped middle? This is the design's second cleverness: while truncating what the model sees, it writes the full output into a managed file. write lands the full text to {global data dir}/tool-output/tool_{ascending ID} (with flag: "wx" exclusive create, guarding against overwrite), and bound puts this file path into the returned outputPaths. So the model gets "a preview + a clue that 'the full text is at this path'":

full output
if over bound →
① head-tail preview to model
+ marker
simultaneously →
② full text spilled to managed file
outputPaths to the model

This step elegantly reconciles the seemingly contradictory goals of "bounded" and "undistorted." The model reads a crisp preview now, not drowned; but should it really need the chopped middle (e.g. to find one specific error line), it has the path—just read that file with lesson 38's read tool (read can offset/limit-page too, perfect for big files). Lay "what the model sees" and "what's actually stored" side by side and this divide-and-conquer is plain:

What the model sees

A head-tail preview (within ~2000 lines / 50 KB) + a marker in the middle + an outputPaths path clue. Small enough to fit the context painlessly.

What's actually stored

A complete, lossless managed file (full text, however large). The model fetches on demand with read, reading whatever section it needs.

It's a beautiful loop: the spilled-out full text is retrievable via the read tool. The whole tool system meshes with itself here—any tool's oversized output is backstopped by another tool (read). This "tools backstopping each other" design is worth savoring: it didn't build ToolOutputStore a dedicated "read back the full text" interface, but reused the already-existing read tool—the full text lands as an ordinary file, and reading files is something the tool system already handles. One fewer wheel reinvented, and two parts mesh seamlessly—exactly the dividend of lesson 36's "one unified tool form": because all tools are isomorphic, they can interconnect and backstop each other like this. And these managed files don't pile up forever: RETENTION = 7 days, auto-cleaned on expiry, not letting the disk grow unboundedly. A bounded view, a complete backup, on-demand retrieval, automatic cleanup—a complete "large-output lifecycle management."

🗺️ The Big Picture

Bounded tool output is the final gate before tool results return to the model, protecting the model's scarce context:

  • Motivation: context is scarce. Tool output can be huge (grep ten-thousand lines, cat megabytes); pouring it raw back burns money, slows things, and pushes the genuinely important out of the window. Lesson 37 settle's resources.bound(...) is this lesson.
  • Head-tail truncation (preview): default MAX_LINES=2000 / MAX_BYTES=50KB (config-overridable); over bound keeps the first half + last half, marker in between—because tool output's info density concentrates at both ends (start=what's running, end=result/error), "keep only first N lines" would chop the all-important result.
  • Spill + retrieve: while truncating, write the full text to a managed file (tool-output/tool_{ascending ID}, flag:"wx" exclusive), put the path in outputPaths for the model; to see the full thing, read it with lesson 38's read tool—bounded view + full backup + on-demand access, the tool system backstopping itself.
  • Distinguish the two bounds: bash's 1 MB memory cap (lesson 39) guards memory; ToolOutputStore's 2000-line/50 KB guards context. RETENTION=7 days auto-cleans managed files.

By here, M7's tool system has one last piece left. Lesson 43 covers the Skills system: a special capability packaging "a body of domain knowledge + a set of operating steps," injected on demand and permissioned—stringing all the prior parts (tool definition, registration, permissions, Context Source) into a higher-level abstraction. After it, M7's "the agent's hands" is fully covered: able to define tools, collect and dispatch them, read/write/search/run/ask, be governed by permissions, hold large outputs in check, and finally package whole skills for reuse—a complete, controllable, extensible tool system.

🔬 Source Detail

The head-tail truncation's core is this "half each" in preview (simplified from tool-output-store.ts):

// over maxLines: keep first half + last half, marker backstops the middle
const headLines = Math.ceil(maxLines / 2)   // head takes one extra (when odd)
const tailLines = Math.floor(maxLines / 2)
const sampled = lines.length <= maxLines
  ? text
  : [ lines.slice(0, headLines).join("\n"),
      lines.slice(lines.length - tailLines).join("\n") ].join("\n")
// when bytes also exceed: takePrefix(headBytes) + takeSuffix(tailBytes), both ends too

Two engineering niceties. One, headLines = ceil, tailLines = floor—when maxLines is odd, the head keeps one more line than the tail, a tiny but telling preference (the start usually merits slightly more context than the end). Two, boundedPreview, when assembling, counts the marker's own bytes into the budget (maxBytes - markerBytes - 4)—it won't "truncate to full quota then paste the marker" and quietly overshoot, but reserves the marker's room before truncating. This rigor of "even the notice's own space counts toward the budget" is the strict landing of "bounded": promised not to exceed 50 KB, so even that "…truncated…" must count within the 50 KB. Bounded is a promise that allows no "close enough."

🎯 Key Takeaways
  • Motivation: the model's context is finite and expensive, tool output can be huge (grep ten-thousand lines/cat megabytes), and pouring it raw back burns money, slows things, and pushes the important out. ToolOutputStore (core/src/tool-output-store.ts) is the final bounding gate before tool results return to the model (lesson 37 settle's resources.bound).
  • Head-tail truncation: default MAX_LINES=2000 / MAX_BYTES=50KB (config/tool-output.ts's max_lines/max_bytes overridable); over bound keeps first half (⌈/2⌉) + last half (⌊/2⌋), marker between—because info density concentrates at both ends (head=what's running, tail=result/error), keep-only-first-N would chop the all-important result. Same for bytes.
  • Spill + retrieve: while truncating, write the full text to a managed file ({global.data}/tool-output/tool_{ascending ID}, flag:"wx" exclusive create), path into outputPaths for the model; to see the full text, read it with lesson 38's read tool (pageable)—bounded view + full backup + on-demand access, the tool system backstopping itself.
  • Two bounds: bash's 1 MB memory cap (lesson 39) guards memory vs ToolOutputStore's 2000-line/50 KB guards context. RETENTION=7 days auto-cleans managed files.
  • Strict bounding: boundedPreview counts the marker's own bytes into the budget (maxBytes - markerBytes - 4), not letting the notice itself overshoot; headLines uses ceil (head one more line than tail when odd). "Bounded" is a promise that allows no "close enough."

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. When ToolOutputStore truncates over-long output, why keep "first half + last half" instead of "only first N lines"?
  1. Purely for symmetric aesthetics
  2. Tool output's info density concentrates at both ends: start=what's running/config, end=result/error/summary, the middle mostly repetitive progress; keep-only-first-N would chop the all-important 'final result/error'
  3. A randomly chosen strategy
  4. Because the tail data is newer
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. preview keeps head headLines=⌈max/2⌉ + tail tailLines=⌊max/2⌋ with a marker between, same half-each for bytes. For logs/tests/dumps, the most useful info is almost always at both ends: start says what's running, end says success or where the error is. Naively "keep only the first 1000 lines" would chop the all-important final result — the model reads progress forever yet doesn't know the outcome. This treats truncation as optimizing "keep as much useful info as possible within a limited budget," not brainlessly chopping to N lines.
2. Truncation inevitably loses info—what about the chopped middle? Can the model still get the full text?
  1. No, the middle is permanently lost
  2. The full text is compressed into one sentence
  3. The full text is emailed to the user
  4. Yes: while truncating, write the full text to a managed file, put the path in outputPaths for the model; to see the full thing, the model reads that file with lesson 38's read tool (pageable)
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. This is the design's second cleverness: while truncating what the model sees, write the full output to {global.data}/tool-output/tool_{ascending ID} (flag:wx exclusive create), and bound puts the path in outputPaths. The model gets "a preview + the full text is at this path," and to see the middle reads that file with the read tool. A beautiful loop — the spilled full text is retrievable via read, the tool system backstopping itself (reusing the existing read, no new interface, exactly lesson 36's unified-tool-form dividend). Bounded view + full backup + on-demand retrieval + 7-day auto-cleanup.
3. Are bash's 1 MB memory cap (lesson 39) and ToolOutputStore's 2000-line/50KB bound (this lesson) the same thing?
  1. Yes, identical
  2. bash's is stricter, supersedes ToolOutputStore
  3. No, two different bounds: bash's guards 'memory' (a command's stdout not piling up to blow the process), ToolOutputStore's guards 'context' (any tool result bounded to 2000 lines/50KB before returning to the model)
  4. Both exist only in config files
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Two different bounds, each guarding its own resource. bash's MAX_CAPTURE_BYTES=1MB is an earlier, lower-level gate guarding against a command's stdout piling up unboundedly in process memory. ToolOutputStore's 2000-line/50KB is the final, model-facing gate guarding against any tool's result blowing the context window before returning to the model. One governs memory safety, the other context budget, cleanly divided. This is M7's recurring 'anywhere volume could be large is bounded' red thread at different layers.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The lesson says ToolOutputStore uses the read tool to "retrieve" the spilled full text—it didn't build a dedicated 'read back the full text' interface but reused the existing read, because the full text lands as an ordinary file and reading files is already handled. This is called "lesson 36's unified-tool-form dividend: because all tools are isomorphic, they can interconnect and backstop each other." Give an example you've seen of "because the interface/data format is unified, modules unexpectedly reuse each other" (Unix everything-is-a-file, pipes). Why is this "composability dividend" of a unified abstraction often unforeseeable beforehand, only emerging afterward?
  • The lesson stresses "bounded is a promise that allows no close-enough": boundedPreview counts even the marker's ('…truncated…') own bytes into the maxBytes budget, not letting the notice itself overshoot. Discuss why this rigor of "counting even the scraps into the budget" matters in resource-constrained scenarios you've worked on (fixed buffers, rate-limit quotas, memory pools). Under what conditions does a "close enough" approximate budget breed a real bug?

The skills system

Through M7 we've dissected the agent's "hands" finely: how to define a tool (L36), collect and dispatch (L37), file/search/exec/network tools (L38–40), permissions (L41), bounded output (L42). This final lesson's Skills system is the closing act that strings all the prior parts into a higher-level abstraction—it answers a very practical question: how do you "teach" an agent a new competence without retraining the model or hardcoding logic into the code? The answer is a "skill": packaging "a body of domain knowledge + a set of operating steps + accompanying scripts/files" into a unit, loaded on demand into the conversation. Want to add a capability to the agent? Just drop a skill into the skill library.

Skills' most exquisite part is its two-stage architecture—this lesson's core, where M5 (System Context) and M7 (tools) meet. A skill is split in two: the "name" half lives resident in the system context (via Context Source, lessons 21–27)—the model always sees which skills exist (just name + a one-line description, very token-cheap); the "body" half loads on demand via a permissioned skill tool (lessons 36/41)—only when a task truly matches a skill does the model call the skill tool to inject that full set of instructions and resources. This is "progressive disclosure / lazy loading": advertise the names cheaply, fetch the body expensively on demand. Grasp this two-stage design and you've understood not just Skills but the core pattern opencode uses again and again—cheaply "advertise" first, expensively "fulfill on demand" later.

📚 Analogy
Picture the skill library as a row of labeled binders on the office wall, each holding a standard operating procedure (SOP) plus a stack of accompanying forms and scripts. Most prominently on the shelf hangs an index card—printing only each binder's spine label (skill name + a one-line note), always in view (this is the system context). The agent, handed a task, first glances at the index card; if a label matches the task at hand, it pulls that binder down and opens it (calls the skill tool)—now the full SOP plus "which folder the supporting materials are in" is spread on the desk. Then the agent follows the SOP step by step, using its usual tools (read to read the referenced scripts, bash to run them) to finish the work. Adding a new competence to the agent isn't by retraining it, but by putting one more binder on the shelf. Index resident, body on demand—this is Skills' two-stage wisdom.

Two stages: names resident, body on demand

First see clearly what a skill consists of—it's far more than "a prompt," but a knowledge package with its own materials:

name
the skill name (the model uses it to call by name)
